Patient Note:
A 34-year-old female with no prior history of thromboembolic disease, chronic neurologic deficit, or limb casting presented to the emergency department this morning complaining of progressive swelling and tightness of her right lower extremity that began two days earlier. She reports that the entire leg, from the groin down to the ankle, has felt “heavy” and appears visibly larger than the opposite side; she noticed difficulty fitting into her usual trousers and shoes despite no recent weight gain. Using a cloth measuring tape at home, she recorded a circumference at mid-calf of 42 cm on the right compared with 38 cm on the left, a difference of approximately 4 cm, and she states that the swelling seems uniform rather than localized to the ankle or foot. Six weeks ago she underwent an elective laparoscopic total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for symptomatic uterine fibroids; the operative note documented an estimated blood loss of 200 mL, and she was discharged on postoperative day two with instructions for early ambulation. She adhered to these instructions and denies having spent prolonged time in bed at home, on a plane, or in a motor vehicle after surgery. She is otherwise healthy, takes only a daily multivitamin, and has no personal or family history of malignancy, clotting disorders, varicose vein procedures, or miscarriage. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, cough, fever, chills, trauma, or recent infections. There has been no calf tenderness when she presses on the muscle bulk herself, but she describes a diffuse dull ache in the thigh when standing for long periods. She has not noticed bulging surface veins, skin discoloration, ulceration, or rashes. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. On examination she is afebrile with a blood pressure of 118/72 mm Hg, pulse 82 bpm, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. She ambulates into the exam room without aid. Inspection of the lower extremities reveals the right leg to be globally enlarged compared with the left; there is fullness of the thigh compartments and the calf with subtle tautness of overlying skin, but no erythema, mottling, or varicosities. Circumferential measurement 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity confirms 41 cm on the right versus 37 cm on the left, consistent with the patient’s report. Popliteal fossa and femoral triangle palpation elicit no focal point tenderness, though the patient notes a generalized sense of pressure when the examiners’ hands encircle the thigh. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) are 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill is less than 2 seconds, and sensation as well as motor strength are intact. There is no pitting edema over the dorsum of the foot. Abdominal examination shows well-healed laparoscopy port scars without hernia or tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Laboratory results obtained at triage demonstrate hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, platelet count 235 k/µL, international normalized ratio 1.0, and creatinine 0.7 mg/dL. A pregnancy test is negative. She has no drug allergies, uses alcohol socially, does not smoke, and denies illicit drug use. Given the uniform swelling of the entire right leg, the documented 4 cm discrepancy in calf circumference, the recent major pelvic surgery within the past two months, and the absence of an alternative explanation that would better account for her presentation, the clinical concern for an acute proximal venous thrombosis is high. Duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity has been ordered along with additional coagulation studies; therapeutic interventions have been deferred pending imaging results.

Patient Note:
A 62-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department this afternoon for evaluation of progressive discomfort in her right calf that started abruptly 24 hours ago while she was seated at home. She described the pain as a constant dull ache that intensifies with ankle dorsiflexion or when she attempts to bear full weight, and she denied antecedent trauma, falls, or new exercise routines. Her history is notable for stage IIIB invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast diagnosed 18 months earlier; she completed neoadjuvant paclitaxel six weeks ago and continues to receive maintenance endocrine therapy under oncology follow-up. Two years ago she suffered a left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke that left her with persistent mild right-sided hemiparesis; as a result, she ambulated with a single-point cane and wore a hinged ankle-foot orthosis but had remained largely independent in activities of daily living. She denied recent abdominal or pelvic operations, and her last hospitalization was more than a year ago for completion mastectomy. Medications include anastrozole, low-dose aspirin for secondary stroke prevention, and sertraline; she does not take hormonal contraceptives or glucocorticoids. She has never smoked, drinks wine socially, and lives with her partner. Review of systems revealed no fevers, chills, night sweats, shortness of breath, palpitations, chest pain, or hematuria. On arrival, blood pressure was 128/74 mm Hg, heart rate 84 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 37.1 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. She appeared comfortable at rest but guarded the right lower limb when shifting on the stretcher. Cardiopulmonary examination was unremarkable with regular rhythm, clear lungs, and no jugular venous distention. Abdominal exam showed a well-healed infra-mammary surgical scar without tenderness, masses, or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities revealed subtle fullness of the right calf, with a circumference of 36 cm measured 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity compared to 34.5 cm on the contralateral side. The thigh and ankle circumferences were symmetric, and the foot appeared normally perfused. Skin over the posteromedial calf was warm but not erythematous, and compression left no appreciable indentation. A web-like network of bluish subcutaneous channels was visibly engorged along the posteromedial aspect of the leg, distinct from typical varicosities. Palpation elicited a vague soreness deep to the gastrocnemius; there was no discrete cord, fluctuance, or crepitus. Active range of motion at the knee was limited mainly by her baseline weakness; passive motion was intact without joint tenderness. Distal pulses were palpable and equal. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, hip pain, or back tenderness. Neurologic exam confirmed chronic right lower-extremity power of 4/5 proximally and 3+/5 distally, unchanged from prior outpatient documentation. Laboratory studies obtained at the bedside included a complete blood count showing hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelet count 282 × 10⁹/L, and white-cell count 6.4 × 10⁹/L. Comprehensive metabolic panel was within normal limits, except for mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase 152 U/L consistent with her oncologic history. Serum D-dimer was not yet resulted at the time of note completion. Plain radiographs of the tibia and fibula ordered to rule out fracture demonstrated only soft-tissue prominence. Point-of-care ultrasound was deferred pending radiology duplex scanning because the department was understaffed. No cutaneous lesions suggested cellulitis, and there was no evidence of ruptured popliteal cyst on initial ultrasound screening performed during residency teaching rounds. Given the acuity of pain onset, visible superficial channels suggestive of collateral flow, the patient’s active malignancy under treatment, and her chronic neurologic weakness necessitating external bracing, there is heightened concern for an occlusive process in the deep venous system of the symptomatic limb. The clinical picture does not currently favor musculoskeletal strain or peripheral neuropathy, and there is no clinical suspicion for arterial insufficiency. The plan includes urgent venous duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity, basic coagulation profile, and discussion with oncology regarding perioperative anticoagulation options should thrombosis be confirmed.

A 29-year-old man with no history of venous thromboembolism, malignancy, or chronic medical conditions arrived to the urgent care ward late this morning complaining of progressive aching and fullness in his right lower leg that began forty-eight hours earlier. He reports that six weeks ago he underwent an open reduction and internal fixation of a displaced left clavicular fracture sustained during a bicycle accident; the operation lasted roughly three hours under general anesthesia, required inpatient monitoring overnight, and was otherwise uncomplicated. He completed a short course of oral opioids and has since returned to desk work as a software engineer without any prolonged convalescence in bed and without use of limb casts or braces. Since surgery he has been generally active, walking his dog and commuting on public transportation daily, and he denies any extended automobile or air travel. Two days ago he first sensed a cramping discomfort in the posterior aspect of the right calf after a routine ten-minute walk from the train station; the pain intensified when he stood for long periods and lessened somewhat when he elevated the leg. By yesterday evening he noticed that the right pant leg felt tight and, concerned, measured the circumference of both calves with a tape he uses at the gym: at a point 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity the right side was 40 cm compared with 36 cm on the left, a discrepancy of 4 cm that persisted this morning and prompted his visit. He denies swelling of the thigh or groin, denies redness or streaking of the skin, and has not observed any rope-like superficial veins. He further denies fever, chills, night sweats, unintended weight loss, trauma to the leg, recent soft-tissue infection, insect bites, or joint pain. There is no chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, palpitations, or syncope. His only medication is 600 mg ibuprofen taken occasionally for shoulder soreness; he takes no hormonal therapy, supplements, or anticoagulants. Family history is negative for clotting disorders, premature cardiovascular disease, or cancer; both parents are alive in their fifties. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On presentation he is alert and in mild discomfort but able to ambulate independently. Vital signs: temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/76 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable with clear breath sounds and a regular cardiac rhythm without murmurs or gallops. Abdominal examination reveals a well-healed 5 cm incision in the left upper quadrant without tenderness or masses. Inspection of the lower extremities shows the right calf to be noticeably fuller than the left, while both thighs and ankles remain symmetric; skin coloration is normal without erythema, cyanosis, or ulcers. No varicose or engorged superficial venous channels are visible. Measurement repeated with a flexible tape confirms a 4 cm difference favoring the right side at the previously described point, whereas thigh circumference measured 15 cm above the patella is identical bilaterally. Palpation along the expected course of the posterior tibial and popliteal veins elicits a distinct, focal tenderness that is absent on the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly itself produces only mild diffuse soreness. There is no Homan sign, though the patient reports a pulling sensation when the ankle is dorsiflexed. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—are 2+ and symmetric, capillary refill is less than two seconds, and sensation and motor strength are intact throughout. There is no palpable cord, no warmth or tenderness over the Achilles tendon, and no crepitus of the knee or ankle joints. Examination of the skin elsewhere shows no rashes or petechiae. Laboratory studies sent prior to any pharmacologic intervention reveal hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL, platelet count 235 k/µL, white blood cell count 6.2 k/µL, serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, AST/ALT within reference range, INR 1.0, and aPTT 30 seconds. A urine toxicology screen is negative, and a rapid antigen test for SARS-CoV-2 is negative. Because the patient exhibits a unilateral calf enlargement greater than 3 cm, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway confined to the calf rather than the entire limb, a recent major operative procedure within the past twelve weeks, and no clinical findings to suggest cellulitis, musculoskeletal injury, ruptured Baker cyst, or other more plausible explanation, the working impression strongly favors an acute thrombus in the proximal calf or popliteal segment of the right lower extremity. Compression duplex ultrasonography of the right femoral-popliteal system has been requested urgently, baseline coagulation parameters have been documented, and the patient has been advised to remain in the department until imaging is completed.

Patient Note:
A 55-year-old man arrived to the urgent care unit late this morning after noticing progressive fullness of his left lower limb over the previous forty-eight hours. He explained that, while showering two days ago, he became aware that the lower half of his left pant leg clung more tightly around the calf than usual; by the following evening the same region felt tense enough to prompt him to measure it with a sewing tape he keeps for tailoring work. The tape read 44 cm at a point 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity on the left compared with 39 cm on the right, a discrepancy of 5 cm that has remained stable since. He denies similar enlargement of the thigh, knee, or ankle and states that his left shoe still fits comfortably, though stepping down first thing in the morning produces a dull ache localized to the posterior calf. There has been no antecedent fall, athletic overexertion, insect bite, or rash, and he has not developed fever, rigors, sweats, or chills. Review of systems is negative for pleuritic chest discomfort, new dyspnea, hemoptysis, palpitations, visual changes, or neurologic deficits. His past history is remarkable for stage III pancreatic adenocarcinoma diagnosed nine months earlier; he is midway through a FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy schedule, having received his most recent infusion nine days ago, and continues to attend oncologic follow-up every two weeks. He takes ondansetron as needed for nausea, a proton-pump inhibitor for reflux, and daily low-dose aspirin for primary cardiovascular prevention but no hormonal agents, glucocorticoids, or erythropoiesis-stimulating drugs. He has never experienced thromboembolic disease, bleeding disorders, or vascular procedures, and screening colonoscopy four years ago was unremarkable. He works part-time from home as a graphic designer, walks his dog around the block twice daily, and drove himself to clinic without assistance. There has been no recent long-haul travel, flight, or automobile journey beyond his usual errand route. He does not smoke, drinks a glass of wine with dinner once or twice weekly, and denies recreational drug use. Family history is negative for premature myocardial infarction, stroke, or hypercoagulable states. On arrival his vital signs were as follows: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 130/76 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats per minute, respiratory rate 15 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. He appeared well hydrated and conversed comfortably. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdominal examination showed a well-healed midline laparotomy scar and mild epigastric tenderness consistent with his known malignancy but no organomegaly or ascites. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated asymmetry isolated to the calf segment: the left gastrocnemius compartment was visibly fuller with subtle sheen of the overlying skin, whereas the thigh circumference measured equivalently on both sides. Gentle finger pressure over the anterior tibia of the left leg produced a 2-mm indentation that rebounded over approximately 20 seconds; no indentation was elicited on the right. Skin temperature was modestly warmer on the affected side yet remained pink without erythema, ecchymosis, or discoloration. The superficial venous pattern on both lower limbs was flat, with no engorged, tortuous subcutaneous channels. Homan sign was not assessed to avoid unnecessary provoking maneuvers. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2 + bilaterally, capillary refill was under two seconds, and light-touch sensation as well as motor strength were symmetric. Knee, ankle, and hip joints moved through full passive and active ranges without crepitus or focal tenderness. No popliteal cystic mass, inguinal lymphadenopathy, or groin hernias were appreciated. Laboratory samples were drawn before any anticoagulant therapy: complete blood count showed hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, white-cell count 5.8 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 268 × 10⁹/L; basic metabolic panel and hepatic function tests were within his recent baseline, and international normalized ratio measured 1.0. Electrocardiography demonstrated normal sinus rhythm. Given the abrupt unilateral calf enlargement exceeding 3 cm, the presence of ipsilateral pitting edema confined to that segment, the patient’s ongoing treatment for an active malignancy, the preserved superficial venous contour, and the absence of an alternative musculoskeletal, dermatologic, or vascular explanation, the working impression favors an acute occlusive process within the deep veins of the symptomatic limb. Duplex ultrasonography of the entire left lower extremity has been requested on an expedited basis; further management will hinge on those imaging results and collaboration with his oncology team regarding systemic anticoagulation versus catheter-directed therapies, taking into account his chemotherapy schedule and bleeding risk profile.

Patient Note:
A 49-year-old woman came to the urgent care area of the medical ward late this morning because of new-onset pain and fullness in her right upper calf that started while she was grocery shopping yesterday afternoon. She describes the discomfort as a deep, pressure-like ache that intensifies when she flexes the knee or when the examiner presses along the line from the medial calf toward the popliteal fossa; she can localize it with one finger and says it is clearly worse when weight-bearing. She denies redness, warmth, itching, fever, chills, or recent skin trauma. She had been back at work as a high-school principal for the last three weeks after undergoing an open sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis for complicated diverticulitis exactly six weeks ago; the postoperative course was uneventful, she was walking the hallways on postoperative day one, and she never required prolonged bed rest at home. Her medical history is notable for a documented iliofemoral venous thrombosis on the opposite side five years earlier that was confirmed by duplex ultrasound and treated with a six-month course of warfarin until complete recanalization was demonstrated. She has no history of malignancy, chronic neurologic deficits, lower-limb casting, pelvic fractures, or recent air travel, and she has remained physically active, walking two miles most evenings with her spouse. Medications include a daily multivitamin, rosuvastatin 10 mg, and ibuprofen as needed for postoperative abdominal wall soreness; she is not taking estrogen-containing therapy, glucocorticoids, or anticoagulants. She quit smoking 10 years ago after a 12-pack-year history and drinks wine on weekends; there is no family history of inherited clotting disorders, premature myocardial infarction, or malignancy. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, or unexplained weight loss. On arrival her vital signs were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/76 mm Hg, heart rate 90 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % breathing room air. She appeared well and was able to stand on the examination stool without assistance. Cardiac auscultation revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; lungs were clear to bases without crackles. The abdominal incision was healed with no tenderness or mass. Examination of the lower limbs showed the right calf to be slightly fuller to the eye but without taut skin or discoloration; when measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity the circumference was 38.5 cm versus 37.2 cm on the left, a discrepancy she said she had not noticed before. Gentle compression over the course of the posterior tibial and popliteal veins elicited focal tenderness, whereas squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from those structures was much less uncomfortable. The overlying skin was warm but dry, and no indentation was produced after five seconds of firm thumb pressure over the tibia. A network of bluish, non-tortuous superficial channels was visibly prominent along the medial aspect of the calf and became more obvious when the leg was placed in a dependent position for several seconds. There were no spider angiomas, no stasis dermatitis, and no varicosities elsewhere. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ bilaterally and symmetric; capillary refill was brisk, and sensorimotor testing of both lower extremities was normal. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, hip tenderness, or back pain. Point-of-care laboratory testing obtained prior to any treatment showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, platelet count 212 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and INR 1.0. Electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm without acute changes. No obvious musculoskeletal or infectious etiology was identified on history or examination, and the examiner judged that other causes of unilateral calf pain—such as cellulitis, muscular strain, Baker cyst rupture, or arterial insufficiency—were less consistent with the physical findings than an occlusion in the deep venous system. She was counseled about signs of pulmonary embolism and remained in the department awaiting formal duplex ultrasonography to confirm the suspected diagnosis.

Patient Note:
A 39-year-old male with a five-year history of moderate ulcerative colitis (UC), as per Montreal classification, was admitted to the emergency department with worsening abdominal and rectal pain secondary to a C. diff infection of one week. Patient had a multi-week history of diarrhea that was waking him up at night leading up to the C. diff diagnosis. One week prior to ED admission, his GI had ordered precautionary leukocytes and C. diff due to history of UC, and both returned positive. Per GI note, "Vancomycin prescribed as this patient has IBD and patients with IBD can develop spontaneous C. diff infections and need to be treated regardless of toxin presence." He had no prior history of antibiotic use over the past three months or triggering factor for C. diff infection.
Over the course of the week, his symptoms progressed, and he claimed to have nausea, vomiting, and hematochezia.
The patient presented with a past medical history of depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, and obstructive sleep apnea. His allergies included trazodone and amoxicillin/clavulanate. His family history was unrevealing. His surgical history included cholecystectomy and thyroglossal duct cyst removal in 2019.
This patient was a nonsmoker. He denied alcohol or drug use. He tried to maintain a healthy diet of lean proteins, fruits, and vegetables.
Initial vitals on presentation included: temperature: 98.6 F, pulse: 90 beats per minute, respiratory rate: 15 breaths per minute, pulse oximetry: 95%, blood pressure: 119/87 mmHg, weight: 217.16 lbs, and body mass index: 32.14.
General surgery was consulted upon CT scan with IV contrast revealing new extraluminal gas in the perineum surrounding the anus and distal rectum (Figures , , , ). He was examined and subsequently diagnosed with rectal perforation.
Preoperatively, the patient’s physical exam revealed an uncomfortable looking young man in mild distress due to abdominal pain. An abdominal exam revealed no distention, masses, rebound, or guarding. The absence of McBurney’s sign, Murphy’s sign, and peritoneal inflammation was noted. There was no active rectal bleeding at the time of physical examination. Laboratory data revealed an elevated c-reactive protein of 66.4. Complete blood count and complete metabolic panel were otherwise within normal limits. Despite infection and imaging reflecting perforation, white blood count was found to be 5.9 k/cmm.
After review of the available data, assessment of clinical condition, and deliberation with the emergency department physicians and the patient’s primary gastroenterology physician, the surgery team decided the patient would require an urgent exploratory laparotomy with total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy due to the diagnosis of rectal perforation secondary to C. diff colitis on chronic UC.
The patient was immediately brought to the operating room. The primary incision was made from the subxiphoid to the pubic symphysis within two hours of initial presentation to the hospital. The colon was visualized and resected; thus, a total colectomy was undergone. The pre-rectal space was opened to identify signs of purulence or injury, which found crepitus and severe UC causing circumferential ulcerations and a deep ulcer in the midline anteriorly. These ulcers traversed all layers of the rectal wall. A drain was placed to drain potential abscess or infection from the pre-rectal space.
The patient left the OR in stable condition after enduring no complications intraoperatively. He remained in the inpatient surgical unit for five days, during which he continued to advance his diet and ambulate successfully. He was discharged after an education session on ostomy bag maintenance and told to follow up with the general surgery team and gastroenterology team in one week. At his one-week follow-up post-op visit, the patient was doing well, ostomy output was draining appropriately, and he denied fever, nausea, or vomiting. No secondary follow-up surgery was required.

Patient Note:
A 78-year-old man was brought to the hospital by his home-health nurse late this morning because of sudden onset enlargement of his left lower extremity that he first noticed while transferring from bed to his recliner the prior evening. He reported that the whole limb—from the groin through the ankle—looked “puffy” and felt heavy when he tried to pivot, forcing him to drag the leg while using his rollator. He denied falling, twisting the knee, sustaining any direct blow, or developing a skin abrasion, and he had not experienced fever, chills, rash, or erysipelas-like warmth. His medical background is notable for metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma diagnosed nine months earlier; he remains on a bi-weekly gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel regimen, with his most recent infusion five days ago, and imaging two months earlier had demonstrated stable hepatic lesions without new disease. Two years ago he suffered an incomplete L4 spinal cord infarction that left him with residual 3+/5 motor strength in the left hip flexors and knee extensors and brisk patellar reflexes; he wears an articulated ankle-foot orthosis during the day but no cast or rigid splint, and although he ambulates slowly he completes short household distances several times per day, including climbing three steps to reach the porch, so he has not been confined to bed or a chair for prolonged periods. He has never had a clot in the legs or lungs, has no known thrombophilia, and takes no anticoagulant medication. His regular drugs include long-acting oxycodone for oncologic pain, metoprolol for rate control of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and a proton-pump inhibitor; he does not use estrogenic compounds, corticosteroids, or erythropoietin stimulators. There is no family history of venous thromboembolism, and he quit smoking 25 years ago. On review of systems he denied new cough, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, palpitations, abdominal distention, urinary retention, or constipation. Vital signs at triage were blood pressure 126/70 mm Hg, heart rate 94 beats/minute and irregularly irregular, respiratory rate 18 breaths/minute, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % while breathing ambient air. He appeared frail but hemodynamically stable, answering questions in full sentences without dyspnea. Cardiac auscultation revealed an irregular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and lung fields were clear to auscultation bilaterally with no dullness on percussion. Abdominal examination showed a well-healed midline laparotomy scar and mild epigastric tenderness related to tumor burden but no organomegaly or ascites. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated a striking global enlargement of the entire left leg compared with the right; the thigh circumference measured at mid-femur was 57 cm on the left versus 52 cm on the right, and the calf circumference taken 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity was 40 cm on the left and 38 cm on the right, yielding a 2 cm discrepancy. The skin over the affected limb was smooth, taut, and slightly warmer than the contralateral side, yet there was no appreciable indentation after firm thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the foot, suggesting absence of meaningful pitting. Color remained normal without streaking or mottling, and no engorged superficial venous channels were visible along the thigh or calf. Palpation elicited a diffuse sense of pressure throughout the limb but did not localize to a cord-like structure along the femoral, popliteal, or posterior tibial tracks; ankle dorsiflexion produced only his baseline neuropathic tightness. Distal perfusion was intact with posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses 2+ bilaterally and capillary refill less than two seconds. Sensory examination showed diminished pinprick below the L4 dermatome on the left, consistent with prior spinal injury, but no new sensory loss. Manual muscle testing revealed unchanged baseline weakness of the left quadriceps while right-sided strength remained 5/5 throughout. Gait could not be assessed during the encounter because he was transported by wheelchair. Laboratory studies drawn before any treatment showed hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, platelet count 268 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.8 × 10⁹/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.1; serum electrolytes and liver enzymes were within his known chronic baseline. A plain radiograph of the femur obtained in the emergency department showed no lytic lesions or fracture that might account for the swelling. Given the unilateral, full-length enlargement of the limb without a convincing musculoskeletal or infectious etiology, the presence of an active malignancy, and chronic paresis of the same extremity, the index of suspicion for an obstructive process within the deep venous system is high, and vascular ultrasound of the entire left leg has been requested; therapeutic decisions will await definitive imaging because of concern for gastrointestinal bleeding risk with anticoagulation in the setting of pancreatic cancer.

Patient Note:
A 28-year-old man with no history of thromboembolic disease, limb casting, neurologic deficit, or recent hospitalization arrived to the urgent assessment unit complaining that his entire left lower extremity had become “puffy and sore” over the preceding forty-eight hours. He reports playing in an amateur weekend rugby match three days earlier during which he sustained multiple tackles, including a direct blow to the lateral thigh that produced an immediate bruise but did not keep him from finishing the game. The following morning he noticed diffuse erythema beginning just above the knee and extending toward the ankle, accompanied by a low-grade subjective fever, and by last night he could feel tightness all the way from the groin to the dorsal aspect of the foot; his jeans were difficult to pull on over the limb, whereas his right trouser leg remained loose. He denies recent long-haul travel, prolonged automobile rides, or remaining in bed for more than his usual seven hours of nightly sleep, and he continues to work at a hardware store where he spends most of the shift standing or climbing ladders. He has never undergone an operative procedure beyond childhood tonsillectomy, takes no prescription medications, does not smoke, and limits alcohol to occasional weekend beers; family history is unremarkable for clotting disorders. He has not experienced chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or cough, and he specifically denies trauma to the calf, insect stings, or animal bites. On arrival he was afebrile at 37.3 °C, blood pressure 124/70 mm Hg, pulse 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 14/min, and oxygen saturation 99 % on room air. Physical examination revealed a well-built male in no acute distress who ambulated into the room without a limp but favored the left leg when pivoting. Inspection showed uniform swelling of the entire limb; circumference taken 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 45 cm on the affected side compared with 40.5 cm on the right, a difference of 4.5 cm. The skin from mid-thigh to mid-calf was dusky red and warm, with scattered ecchymoses corresponding to his reported tackle injuries, but there were no engorged superficial channels or varicosities appreciated. Palpation of the groin, femoral triangle, popliteal fossa, posterior calf, and along the course of the peroneal compartment did not provoke focal cord-like tenderness; instead, the patient described a generalized aching sensation reminiscent of a deep bruise. There was full active range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle, though he winced when the quadriceps contusion was compressed. Distal dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ and symmetric, capillary refill less than two seconds, and sensation in the foot was intact to light touch and pinprick. No discrete fluctuance, bullae, or crepitus were present, and the inguinal nodes were not enlarged. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations were normal, abdomen was soft and nontender, and there was no peripheral edema on the contralateral limb, upper extremities, or face. Bedside laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 12.6 × 10⁹/L with 80 % neutrophils, hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 1.0; C-reactive protein was elevated at 6.4 mg/dL. Plain radiographs of the femur and tibia showed soft-tissue swelling without fracture. The combination of widespread erythema, elevated inflammatory markers, and a clear history of blunt trauma suggests an infectious or inflammatory process, such as cellulitis or post-contusional hematoma, that could plausibly account for the patient’s presentation; nonetheless, because of the measurable calf asymmetry exceeding 3 cm and the diffuse swelling that involves the whole extremity in an otherwise healthy, ambulatory adult with no prior clot, an occlusive event within the deep venous system remains under consideration. Duplex ultrasound of the left lower limb has been ordered, and definitive therapy will be deferred pending imaging results and discussion with the patient regarding the risks and benefits of empiric interventions.

Patient Note:
A 38-year-old man was transported by ambulance to the hospital after noticing marked enlargement of his right lower leg when he attempted to stand from his couch earlier this evening. Five days ago he developed a febrile viral gastroenteritis that left him profoundly fatigued and he spent the better part of the past four days reclined on a sofa, rising only for brief bathroom visits and minimal meals; he returned to a standing position for the first time this afternoon and immediately perceived a heavy, tight sensation in the posterior calf. He denies any recent falls, direct trauma, twisting injury, insect bites, rashes, or strenuous exercise. His medical history includes mild intermittent asthma controlled with albuterol as needed and no prior thromboembolic episodes, and he has never required orthopedic casting, braces, or limb immobilization. He has not undergone any operative procedures, catheterizations, or endoscopies in the past year, takes no prescription medications other than an as-needed inhaler, and uses no hormonal agents or corticosteroids. He last traveled by air more than six months ago and works remotely as a software engineer. There is no family history of thrombophilia, early myocardial infarction, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease. He does not smoke, drinks beer socially, and denies illicit drug use. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, palpitations, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, dysuria, or fevers since the initial viral illness subsided two days ago. On arrival he appeared mildly anxious but in no respiratory distress. Vital signs: temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 per minute, oxygen saturation 99 % on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear to auscultation with good air movement bilaterally. Abdominal examination showed soft, nontender bowel segments with normal active sounds and no hepatosplenomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated visible fullness confined to the right calf: a flexible tape placed 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity measured 38.5 cm on the right and 34.8 cm on the left, a disparity of 3.7 cm. The overlying skin was warm and exhibited a lattice of engorged, blue-tinged superficial vessels tracing obliquely from the medial gastrocnemius toward the popliteal crease, distinct from his baseline and without the tortuosity characteristic of varicosities. Skin color was otherwise preserved with no erythema or streaking. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia produced no persistent indentation, and the dorsum of the foot was similarly non-pitting. Palpation of the femoral triangle, popliteal fossa, posterior calf, and course of the peroneal compartments elicited no focal tenderness; he described a diffuse dull ache that intensified when he attempted to dorsiflex the ankle against resistance. Range of motion at the knee and ankle was full though movement augmented the sense of tightness. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) were palpable and symmetric, capillary refill was brisk under two seconds, and sensory examination to light touch, pinprick, and vibration was intact. Motor strength was 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy or joint effusion, and the contralateral limb appeared normal. Laboratory studies drawn before any anticoagulation showed hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, platelet count 268 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 7.2 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and normal transaminases; coagulation profile revealed a PT of 12.0 seconds with an INR of 1.0 and an aPTT of 29 seconds. A plain radiograph of the tibia and fibula ordered to exclude occult fracture showed only soft-tissue prominence without bony abnormality. No clinical features pointed toward cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, muscle tear, or lymphedema as an equally compelling explanation for the findings. In light of the objective calf asymmetry exceeding three centimeters, the network of newly distended superficial channels suggesting collateral venous flow, the absence of dependent pitting, his prolonged recumbency over the recent days, and lack of prior thrombotic history or recent surgical intervention, an occlusive process within the deep venous system of the right lower limb is considered highly suspicious and warrants immediate duplex ultrasonographic evaluation.

Patient Note:
A 38-year-old postpartum woman arrived to the ambulatory triage area late this afternoon because her left lower leg had become noticeably heavier and mildly painful over the preceding 24 hours. Six weeks ago she delivered her second child via uncomplicated scheduled cesarean section that required spinal anesthesia and an overnight stay; she was discharged on postoperative day 2 and has since resumed routine household activities without assistive devices. Yesterday morning, while standing at the kitchen counter for an extended period preparing bottles, she felt a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the calf that worsened when she bore weight and eased modestly with elevation. By evening she observed that her sock left an imprint on that side but not on the right. She denies antecedent trauma, ankle sprain, insect bite, recent intense exercise, or local skin infection. She also denies fevers, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, or constitutional complaints. There is no history of malignant disease, hematologic disorder, or prior venous clot, nor has she ever required anticoagulation. She reports no neurologic illness, limb weakness, or application of a plaster cast, and she has remained fully mobile caring for her newborn. She uses no estrogen-containing contraception and takes only prenatal vitamins and 600 mg ibuprofen as needed for incisional soreness. Both parents are alive and free of venous thromboembolic events; no siblings have clotting disorders. She has never smoked, drinks wine rarely, and denies illicit drug use. On presentation she appeared comfortable holding the infant carrier, spoke in complete sentences, and ambulated without assistance. Vital signs before any medications were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm with no murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation. Abdominal inspection showed a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar that was nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated subtle fullness confined to the left calf; there was no erythema, hyperpigmentation, varicosity, or brawny skin change. A flexible tape placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38.0 cm on the left and 36.4 cm on the right, a disparity of 1.6 cm. Circumference at mid-thigh and around the ankle was symmetric. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the left foot produced a 1+ indentation that resolved within 15 seconds, whereas identical pressure on the right left no depression. Palpation along the course of the popliteal vein and posteromedial calf elicited focal discomfort described by the patient as “a deep bruise,” compared with no tenderness over the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from the neurovascular bundle produced only minimal diffuse soreness. There was no palpable subcutaneous cord and no crepitus. Passive dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain at the Achilles insertion. Skin temperature was marginally warmer over the symptomatic calf compared with the right when assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2+ bilaterally, and capillary refill of all toes was under two seconds. Motor strength in ankle dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and great-toe extension was 5/5, and sensation to light touch and vibration was intact. Straight-leg raise and seated slump test were negative for radicular symptoms, making a spinal etiology improbable. There was no ipsilateral knee effusion, meniscal tenderness, or ligamentous laxity, and range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full. No inguinal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. Basic laboratory studies obtained prior to any antithrombotic therapy showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, white blood cells 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.0 with activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. These values reveal no anemia, thrombocytopenia, or renal impairment that might confound subsequent management. Given the recent abdominal-pelvic surgical intervention within the past two months, the presence of unilateral pitting edema confined to the calf, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway, and the absence of a compelling musculoskeletal, dermatologic, or neurologic explanation, an acute thrombotic obstruction of the deep venous system remains the leading consideration at this stage of evaluation.

Patient Note:
A 47-year-old man with a history limited to well-controlled mild asthma and seasonal rhinitis presented to the ambulatory triage area mid-morning complaining of an aching sensation and progressive enlargement of his right calf that began roughly thirty-hours prior while he was at work installing cabinetry in a two-story townhome. He recalls kneeling frequently and carrying heavy toolboxes up a flight of stairs but denies any single twisting injury, audible pop, or sharp pain; instead, he noticed a vague tightness behind the knee late that evening which intensified overnight, prompting him to inspect both lower limbs side-by-side in the bathroom mirror. The following morning his work sock left a noticeable indentation at the upper ankle and the posterior calf looked fuller than its counterpart, so he measured the circumference with a flexible sewing tape he keeps for home projects: at a point exactly 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity the right leg measured 41 cm whereas the left measured 37 cm, a disparity of 4 cm that persisted when rechecked two hours later before clinic arrival. He reports no comparable swelling of the thigh, knee, or foot, no discoloration, and no warmth appreciable through his trouser leg. He has not been bedridden—he walks his dog twice daily, drove himself to the clinic, and continues normal activities except for avoiding squats because they provoke discomfort. He has not undergone surgery, endoscopy, or invasive dental work in the past year, and the most recent hospitalization was an overnight observation for an asthma exacerbation four years ago. He has never received chemotherapy, has no known malignancy, and has not required a cast, splint, or immobilizing boot. He flew to Denver on a two-hour flight last week but sat in an aisle seat and walked the terminal without prolonged stasis. He denies fever, chills, sweats, rash, trauma, insect bite, recent soft-tissue infection, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, hemoptysis, or syncope. Medications include inhaled budesonide-formoterol twice daily and an albuterol rescue inhaler used perhaps once a month; he takes no hormonal preparations, anticoagulants, or herbal supplements. He never smokes, drinks two beers on weekends, and does not use illicit drugs. There is no family history of thromboembolic disease or hypercoagulable states. On examination he appears fit, ambulating with a slight antalgic gait favoring the right side but otherwise in no distress. Vital signs: temperature 98.4 °F (36.9 °C), pulse 88 beats/min regular, blood pressure 128/74 mm Hg, respiratory rate 14/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiac auscultation reveals a normal S1/S2 without murmurs; lungs are clear with no wheezes; jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Inspection of the lower extremities shows asymmetric fullness confined to the mid-calf region of the right leg with a subtle glossy sheen of overlying skin; the right ankle and foot appear normal in size, and thigh girth measured 15 cm proximal to the patella is symmetric at 50 cm bilaterally. Gentle finger pressure over the anterior tibia of the right calf produces a 2-mm pit that rebounds within 15 seconds, whereas no indentation forms on the left. The right calf feels slightly warmer but not erythematous. Palpation along the course of the popliteal and posterior tibial venous channels elicits focal tenderness deep to the medial gastrocnemius; squeezing the muscle belly itself produces only diffuse soreness. There is no palpable cord, no varicosities, and no cyanosis of the toes. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ and equal; capillary refill is less than two seconds. Range of motion at the knee and ankle is full; dorsiflexion of the ankle with knee extended reproduces a pulling discomfort but no lancinating pain. Neurologic examination shows intact sensation and 5/5 motor strength. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is present. Bedside urine dip is unremarkable. Baseline laboratory tests obtained before any anticoagulant therapy demonstrate hemoglobin 15.0 g/dL, platelet count 242 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.92 mg/dL, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, AST/ALT within normal limits, prothrombin time 12.1 seconds with an INR of 1.0, and aPTT 29 seconds. An electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm. Clinically, the unilateral calf enlargement greater than 3 cm, discrete pitting edema confined to the symptomatic limb, and pinpoint tenderness tracking the deep venous pathway raise strong concern for an obstructive process within the deep veins of the right calf; musculoskeletal strain or ruptured popliteal cyst remain possibilities but appear less compelling given the objective girth discrepancy and focal venous tenderness. Duplex ultrasonography of the entire right lower extremity has been ordered on an urgent basis, and the patient has been instructed to limit strenuous activity and to alert staff immediately should he notice sudden chest discomfort or worsening shortness of breath while awaiting imaging.

Patient Note:
A 41-year-old man was evaluated in the acute care treatment room late this morning after noticing progressive heaviness and tightness in his left lower calf that began the previous evening while he was watching television with his family. He reports that six weeks earlier he sustained a nondisplaced distal tibial fracture while playing recreational soccer; a fiberglass long-leg cast extending from just below the groin to the metatarsal heads was applied in the emergency department that same night and remained in place until five days ago, when orthopedics removed it and fitted him with a walking boot. Since cast removal he has been fully weight-bearing as tolerated, climbing the stairs in his two-story home several times daily, and he denies having spent any prolonged period confined to bed or a chair. He works as a graphic designer and returned to on-site duties two weeks ago, spending most of the day seated but able to get up to stretch and visit colleagues. Yesterday afternoon, while switching from the boot to a supportive sneaker, he spotted subtle fullness of the posterior calf just above the Achilles insertion; by bedtime the leg felt “stuffed into a sock one size too small,” though he could still ambulate without limping. This morning he woke to find the circumference of the left calf larger than the right at a glance; using a flexible dressmaker’s tape he keeps in his desk, he measured 43 cm on the symptomatic side versus 38 cm on the contralateral limb at a point 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity, a difference of 5 cm that prompted him to seek evaluation. He characterizes the discomfort as dull pressure rather than sharp pain, rated 3/10 at rest and 5/10 when standing for more than a minute, and notes that it partially improves on elevation. He denies focal tenderness when he presses the groin or the crease behind the knee, and states that squeezing the calf muscle itself feels more like post-workout soreness than true pain. He has not observed redness, streaking, or skin breakdown; finger pressure over the anterior tibia leaves a shallow indentation that slowly reforms over approximately 15 seconds, whereas the right leg shows no such depression. He has no fever, chills, night sweats, or weight loss; no shortness of breath, chest discomfort, palpitations, syncope, or cough; and no recent insect bites, rash, or penetrating injury. Past medical history is remarkable only for the aforementioned fracture and well-controlled mild asthma for which he uses an albuterol inhaler infrequently during spring pollen season. He has never been told he had a blood clot, and there is no family history of thromboembolic disease, early myocardial infarction, or stroke. He takes no prescription medications other than a daily 10 mg montelukast tablet and occasional ibuprofen 400 mg for ankle soreness; he is not on hormonal therapy, steroids, or anticoagulation. He denies cigarette smoking, drinks two to three beers on weekends, and has never used illicit drugs. He completed a two-hour automobile trip to visit relatives last weekend but has not flown or traveled long distances recently, and he has not undergone any operation in the last year. Review of systems is otherwise negative. On examination he is alert, oriented, and in no acute distress, able to stand and walk across the room unaided. Vital signs: temperature 37.2 °C, blood pressure 122/78 mm Hg, heart rate 86 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs and clear breath sounds bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, nontender, without organomegaly. Inspection of lower extremities shows the left calf visibly fuller with a slight shiny appearance of the skin; color is pink and comparable to the right. No cord-like superficial vessels or varicosities are apparent, and there is no ecchymosis. A repeat measurement by staff at the same 10 cm landmark confirms 43 cm on the left and 38 cm on the right. Thigh circumference measured 15 cm above the superior patellar border is symmetric at 52 cm bilaterally. Gentle palpation along the course of the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, and posterior tibial groove produces no focal tenderness; compression over the gastrocnemius belly elicits only diffuse mild soreness. Skin temperature is minimally warmer on the affected side, and 2 mm of pitting is reproducible over the anterior tibia; the right leg remains non-pitting. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ and equal, capillary refill is less than two seconds, and neurologic testing shows intact sensation with 5/5 motor strength. Passive ankle dorsiflexion does not provoke abrupt pain, and there is full, pain-free range of motion of the knee and hip. No inguinal lymphadenopathy, groin masses, or popliteal cysts are detected. Portable ankle radiographs from cast removal showed interval fracture healing without hardware, and he has undergone no imaging today prior to assessment. Laboratory specimens drawn before any therapy reveal hemoglobin 15.0 g/dL, platelet count 230 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 7.0 × 10⁹/L, creatinine 0.93 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.0; hepatic transaminases are within reference limits. Given the recent immobilization in a plaster device, the measurable unilateral calf enlargement exceeding 3 cm, pitting confined to that limb, absence of superficial venous collaterals, lack of tenderness over named deep venous channels, and no convincing alternative explanation such as cellulitis or muscle tear, vascular imaging of the left lower extremity has been arranged on an urgent basis while laboratory results are finalized and baseline coagulation parameters recorded.

Patient Note:
A 63-year-old woman with a past medical history limited to diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and well-controlled hypertension walked into the emergency department this afternoon complaining that her entire right lower limb had progressively enlarged over the last thirty-six hours. She first noticed the change yesterday morning when her ankle strap sandals felt unusually tight and she had difficulty sliding her pant leg past the mid-thigh; by evening she required help from her partner to remove the shoe, and when she rose from bed today the leg felt heavy enough that climbing the three steps to her porch was laborious. She denies antecedent trauma, insect bite, recent soft-tissue infection, skiing or hiking incidents, vigorous exercise, or musculoskeletal strain. She has not had fever, chills, sweats, skin warmth, or erythema, and she specifically denies low-grade back pain, flank discomfort, or groin masses. There is no shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, palpitations, dizziness, abdominal pain, or urinary complaints. She works full-time as a bookstore manager, is on her feet most of the day, and commutes by city bus; she has not been confined to bed or a chair, nor has she taken any car or air trips longer than one hour during the past month. The patient has never undergone orthopedic casting, lower-extremity splinting, venous ablation, or vascular bypass, and her last surgical procedure was an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy eight years earlier. She recalls no personal or family history of clots in the legs or lungs and takes lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and a daily multivitamin; she does not use estrogen-containing medication, corticosteroids, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or antiplatelet drugs. She quit cigarette smoking twenty years ago after a 5-pack-year exposure, drinks two glasses of wine on weekends, and denies illicit drug use. On arrival she was alert, in no acute distress but visibly concerned. Vital signs before any pharmacologic therapy were temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 128/78 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats/minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed clear lung fields bilaterally and a regular S1/S2 without murmurs, gallops, or rubs. The abdomen was soft and nontender with active bowel sounds and no palpable masses. Inspection of the lower extremities showed striking asymmetry: the right limb appeared globally swollen from groin to ankle with a subtle glossy sheen of the skin, whereas the left limb maintained its usual contour. A tape measure placed 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity recorded a circumference of 41 cm on the right versus 37 cm on the left, a 4 cm discrepancy; at mid-femur the right measured 56 cm and the left 49 cm, confirming swelling that encompasses the entire length of the limb rather than being isolated to one segment. Over the anterior tibia of the right leg, firm thumb pressure produced a 3 mm depression that refilled in approximately 25 seconds; no indentation was produced on the contralateral side. The skin temperature of both calves and feet was equivalent by touch, and coloration remained pink without warmth, erythema, or streaking. No raised, tortuous, or engorged superficial venous channels were visible along the thigh, popliteal region, or calf, and gentle palpation in those regions did not reveal a palpable cord or focal tenderness. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ bilaterally, capillary refill was less than two seconds, and sensation to light touch as well as motor strength graded 5/5 throughout. The knee, ankle, and hip joints moved through full active and passive ranges without crepitus or discomfort. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, no femoral or popliteal bruit, and no signs of lymphedema such as peau d’orange or thickened dorsal foot skin. Laboratory studies drawn prior to any anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy revealed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 243 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 7.1 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 0.88 mg/dL, AST 21 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, INR 1.0, and aPTT 29 seconds; a urine β-hCG was negative. Plain radiographs of the femur and tibia ordered by the triage physician were normal, showing no fracture or periosteal reaction. Given the acute, unilateral, full-length swelling with a measured calf circumference difference exceeding 3 cm, the presence of pitting confined to the symptomatic limb, absence of engorged superficial collaterals, preservation of arterial perfusion, and lack of recent immobilization, surgery, or an obvious infectious or musculoskeletal explanation, an obstructive process within the deep venous system of the right lower extremity is of primary concern, and emergent duplex ultrasonography of the iliac, femoral, popliteal, and calf veins has been requested to clarify the diagnosis before initiating definitive therapy.

Patient Note:
A 42-year-old woman with no history of venous thromboembolism, malignancy, chronic heart or kidney disease, or connective-tissue disorders walked into the emergency department this afternoon because her entire right lower extremity had felt “heavy and bloated” since the previous evening. She recalls stepping out of her car after a thirty-minute commute from work yesterday when she noticed her right shoe felt snugger than usual; by the time she prepared for bed the fullness had crept from the ankle to the groin, making her jeans difficult to remove and leaving a faint impression of her sock cuff around the malleolus. She denies fever, chills, sweats, rigors, loss of appetite, unintended weight change, insect bites, trauma, or recent skin abrasions. She also denies chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, shortness of breath, palpitations, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, flank pain, or neurological deficits. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable for rash, joint swelling, urinary urgency, or menstrual abnormalities. Five weeks earlier she underwent an uncomplicated laparoscopic total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy for symptomatic leiomyomas; the procedure lasted two and a half hours under general anesthesia, required a single overnight stay, and she was ambulating in the ward the same evening. Since discharge she has resumed her job as a receptionist at a recreational vehicle dealership, alternating between standing at the front counter and walking the showroom; she has not required assistive devices or prolonged periods in bed and she completed the prescribed postoperative incentive-spirometry and ambulation goals without issue. She has taken only acetaminophen 650 mg as needed for incisional discomfort and a daily combined calcium–vitamin D supplement; she is on no hormonal therapy, corticosteroids, anticoagulants, or erythropoiesis-stimulating agents. Her last long-distance travel was a two-hour flight three months ago for a family reunion. She never smoked, consumes wine socially once or twice per month, and does not use recreational drugs. Both parents are alive without cardiovascular or thrombotic disease, and no siblings or children have experienced clots or bleeding disorders. On arrival she appeared well and conversed comfortably but was concerned about the asymmetry of her legs. Vital signs at triage: temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 86 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs were clear to auscultation and percussion. The abdomen was soft with healed 1-cm trocar scars in the infra-umbilical and suprapubic regions, nontender and without palpable masses. Examination of the lower extremities showed a noticeable enlargement of the entire right limb from mid-thigh to dorsum of the foot; the mid-femoral circumference measured 54 cm on the right and 50 cm on the left, while 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38 cm on the right and 36.5 cm on the left. The overlying skin was warm and taut but retained normal color without streaking, ecchymosis, or ulceration. When steady thumb pressure was applied for five seconds over the anterior tibia, a 3-mm indentation slowly refilled over approximately 15 seconds; no such indentation could be produced on the contralateral limb. There were no distended subcutaneous venous channels, varicosities, or palpable cords along the course of the great saphenous, femoral, popliteal, or posterior tibial veins. Gentle palpation of those tracks elicited only a diffuse sense of tightness rather than focal tenderness, and dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain. Hip, knee, and ankle joints moved through full active and passive range without crepitus or guarding. Distal pulses—posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis—were 2+ bilaterally with brisk capillary refill; sensation to light touch, vibration, and pinprick was intact, and motor strength was 5/5 throughout both lower limbs. The inguinal lymph nodes were not enlarged, and there was no groin hernia. There were no signs of cellulitis, lymphedema from prior lymph-node dissection, musculoskeletal strain, or ruptured Baker cyst. Point-of-care laboratory tests obtained before any therapy revealed hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.4 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 243 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.84 mg/dL, electrolytes within reference range, international normalized ratio 1.0, activated partial thromboplastin time 28 s, and C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/dL. Electrocardiography displayed normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes. A plain radiograph of the right femur ordered to exclude occult fracture showed only soft-tissue fullness. Given the acute, unilateral, whole-limb swelling with demonstrable pitting confined to that leg, the recent pelvic surgery performed within the past six weeks, the absence of visible collateral cutaneous veins, and the lack of a more compelling musculoskeletal, infectious, or inflammatory explanation, there is substantial concern for obstruction within the deep venous system of the right lower extremity. Compression duplex ultrasonography of the entire right leg has been requested urgently, and baseline laboratory values were documented before consideration of anticoagulation.

Patient Note:

She explained that two weeks earlier she was admitted to a community hospital for an influenza-associated pneumonia that required intravenous antibiotics and supplemental oxygen; although she never needed ventilatory support, she remained largely confined to bed in a monitored room for five consecutive days and afterwards spent another four days at home reclining on a couch because of lingering fatigue. She finally felt strong enough yesterday to attempt household chores and, upon standing, immediately perceived a dragging heaviness in the entire left leg from groin to ankle, accompanied by a dull ache that intensified when she tried to climb stairs. She denied any isolated trauma to the hip, thigh, knee, or calf, and has not noticed erythematous streaking, pruritus, insect bites, fluctuance, or ulcers. There was no antecedent long-haul automobile or air travel, she has not worn an orthopedic boot or cast, and she retains full voluntary movement of both legs without sensory loss or weakness. Past medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes and stage 1 hypertension, managed respectively with metformin 1000 mg twice daily and lisinopril 10 mg every morning; she has never experienced venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, myocardial infarction, or congestive heart failure. She underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy eight years ago and a screening colonoscopy last year, and there have been no operative procedures, endoscopies, or invasive joint injections since. Medications do not include estrogen preparations, glucocorticoids, selective SERMs, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or anticoagulants. She quit smoking fifteen years ago after a 20-pack-year history, drinks one glass of wine on special occasions, and has never used illicit drugs. Family history is negative for hereditary coagulopathies, premature coronary disease, or malignancy. Review of systems elicited no fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss, pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis, palpitations, abdominal distension, or urinary complaints. Upon arrival her vital signs were temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 138/82 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats/minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. She sat upright without respiratory distress and conversed in full sentences. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear to auscultation and percussion throughout. The abdomen was soft with normoactive bowel sounds and no organomegaly. Examination of the lower extremities was remarkable for striking asymmetry: the left limb, from the proximal thigh to the dorsum of the foot, appeared uniformly enlarged with a glossy sheen to the skin; the thigh circumference at mid-femur exceeded that of the right by several centimeters, and the calf and ankle were similarly prominent, though an exact tape measurement was deferred until after imaging to avoid unnecessarily manipulating the limb. A lattice of bluish, non-tortuous superficial channels was conspicuous along the medial thigh and posterior calf and became more distended when the leg was placed in a dependent position for thirty seconds. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia produced an immediate rebound with no lasting indentation, and comparable pressure over the contralateral side yielded identical findings, indicating the swelling was not of a pitting character. Palpation of the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, and posterior compartment elicited a vague pressure sensation but no discrete focal tenderness or palpable cord. Knee and ankle joints moved through full active and passive ranges without crepitus, effusion, or localized warmth. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill was less than two seconds, and cutaneous sensation to light touch and pinprick remained intact bilaterally. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, no groin masses, and the contralateral limb was completely normal in contour, temperature, and color. Point-of-care laboratory studies drawn prior to any pharmacologic intervention revealed hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, platelet count 254 × 10⁹/L, white-cell count 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, and an international normalized ratio of 1.0; liver enzymes were within reference limits. An electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm at 86 beats/minute without ST-T abnormalities. The history of prolonged recumbency exceeding three days, the global enlargement of the entire symptomatic extremity, the emergence of conspicuous collateral superficial veins, the absence of pitting edema, absence of neurologic impairment or casting, and the lack of any recent surgery, cellulitis, lymphedema, or musculoskeletal injury that could equally account for the presentation make an intraluminal obstructive process within the deep venous system the leading consideration, and a duplex ultrasound from the iliac segment to the ankle has been requested on an urgent basis while anticoagulation decisions await imaging confirmation.

There is no personal history of cerebrovascular accident or transient neurologic deficits, and no episodes of claudication, limb ischemia, or heart failure decompensation have been documented. Additional medical problems include stage 3a chronic kidney disease attributed to hypertensive nephrosclerosis, mild chronic obstructive pulmonary disease related to remote tobacco use (quit fifteen years ago, 30 pack-year history), and controlled essential hypertension managed with lisinopril and amlodipine. The patient consumes minimal alcohol, denies illicit drug use, and lives independently with his spouse. Family history is significant for coronary disease in both parents in their 60s but none for aneurysmal disease. Vital signs in clinic: blood pressure 138/72 mm Hg, heart rate 58 beats/min in sinus rhythm, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C, body mass index 27 kg/m². Abdomen is soft and nontender with a palpable expansile mass in the midline; no bruit is appreciated. Extremities show no edema; capillary refill is brisk. Pre-operative laboratory data obtained this morning demonstrate hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40%, white blood cell count 6.1 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 210 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 34 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL, which is consistent with his documented baseline over the previous six months. On examination, blood pressure is 134/76 mmHg, pulse 72 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 per minute, oxygen saturation 97 % on room air, and temperature 36.8 °C. She is alert, in no distress, and speaking full sentences. Jugular venous pressure is at the angle of the mandible with the patient at 45 degrees. There is a laterally displaced apical impulse and a soft S3; no murmurs or pericardial rub are present. Bibasilar crackles are absent, lungs are clear, and there is trace bilateral ankle pitting. Abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant without rebound or guarding; Murphy sign is negative today. No focal neurological deficits are elicited. Pre-operative laboratory studies drawn this morning reveal hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, white blood cell count 8.1 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.2 mg/dL, unchanged from prior baselines; estimated glomerular filtration rate calculates to 54 mL/min/1.73 m². Fasting glucose is 128 mg/dL and HbA1c measured two weeks ago at her endocrinology visit was 7.4 %. Chest radiograph shows a mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette without pulmonary congestion, and an electrocardiogram demonstrates normal sinus rhythm with QRS duration 96 ms and no ischemic changes. Functional capacity is estimated at four to five metabolic equivalents based on her ability to do light household work and walk three to four blocks on level ground. After review of the clinical status, cardiac medications are to be continued through the perioperative period, morning insulin dose will be reduced to 50 % and administered with dextrose infusion as per protocol, and anesthesiology has been informed of her ventricular function. No additional cardiac imaging or stress testing is deemed necessary at this stage; the patient is instructed on pre-operative fasting and to present to the surgical center two hours before the scheduled start time.

Patient Note:
A 56-year-old man was transferred to nephrology department due to recurrent episodes of fever (38°C～39.2°C), cough, sputum, and thoracalgia for the past three months. The patient had been admitted previously to a local hospital where computer tomography (CT) revealed pleural effusion in both lungs. Laboratory tests showed serum creatinine (sCr) 221.9 μmol/L; proteinuria (3+); and 24 hour proteinuria 5.3g. The patient was diagnosed with chronic renal insufficiency, and pulmonary infection. He was treated with antibiotics for pulmonary infection, but infection-related symptoms gradually worsened and serum creatinine levels reached 434μmol/L within one week. Subsequently, the patient was transferred to our unit for further treatment.
On admission, physical examination showed T 38°C; R, 18 bpm; HR, 99 bpm; and BP 124/60 mmHg. Laboratory tests found hemoglobin (Hb) 81 g/L; WBC 18.27x109/L, ESR 49.0 mm/H, and sCr 611.0 μmol/L; Urea 22.90 mmol/L; antinuclear antibody (ANA) (−); extractable nucler antigen (ENA) (−); C-reactive protein (CRP) 231mg/L; IgG 10.1g/L; IgA 1650.0 mg/L; IgM 345.0 mg/L; IgE 227.95 IU/mL; RBC 30~40/HPF in the 
; and 24 hour proteinuria 2.9 g/L. Ultrasound examination of the urologic system showed enlarged kidneys. Additional laboratory tests found P-ANCA (1:10) (+); ANCA-MPO (+); anti-GBM antibody (−). The patient was diagnosed with ANCA-associated vasculitis, AAV associated glomerulonephritis and put on CVVH and antibiotic therapy. After one week, the patient’s infection-related symptoms gradually improved, and the patient was put on intravenous methylprednisolone therapy (80 mg/d).
Four days later, however, the patient developed melana mixed with bloody stool. Laboratory tests revealed Hb 87g/L, Cr 586.9 umol/L, PLT 355×109/L, Fib 2.48 g/L, PT 17.8s, Antithrombin III 92.2%. The gastroscopy and colonoscopy were normal. And capsule endoscopy revealed no obvious abnormalities, including bleeding of mucous membrane. Conventional therapy of GI bleeding (PPI and somatostatin) was ineffective. Finally selective mesenteric arterial angiography was performed, which indicated a hemorrhagic spot in a branch of the inferior mesenteric artery (). Selective transarterial embolization was performed with steel coils (Cook, Bloomington, USA.) (). After the procedure, GI bleeding was completely alleviated. However, two weeks later, the patient presented with hematochezia again and selective transarterial angiography was performed repeatedly, which revealed another hemorrhagic spot in a branch of IMA, and subsequently arterial embolization with coils was performed again (, ).
After TAE, the symptoms improved and three days later, the repeated fecal-occult-blood tests were negative, but the vasculitis was not completely controlled for the other symptoms were still existed. The patient was treated with a course of intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy (500 mg/d) followed by a course of intravenous cyclophosphamide (CTX) pulse therapy (600mg). Moreover, the patient underwent repeated plasma exchange (8 sections), blood products transfusion, cryoprecipitate therapy, and intravenous gamma globulin infusion (20 g/d for 5 days).
