Patient Note:
A 63-year-old Thai man, with a long-standing history of alcohol and tobacco use, was hospitalized with a two-month history of progressive jaundice, generalized pruritus, and significant involuntary weight loss. He reported no fever, abdominal pain, or vomiting. For the 4 weeks prior to his admission, he also suffered from progressive fatigue, poor oral intake, pale stools, and decreased urine output. His past medical history was significant for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and stage 3A chronic kidney disease (baseline serum creatinine of 1.5 mg/dL, estimated glomerular filtration rate of 48.8 mL/min/1.73 m2). His family history was negative for any kidney disease or malignancy. He had no history of herbal medicine use, intravenous drug abuse, tattoos, or blood transfusions. On physical examination, he was alert and oriented with severe jaundice, a distended abdomen with an enlarged liver, and a 1+ bilateral pitting edema of his lower extremities. He had no stigmata of chronic liver disease. The rest of the physical examination was unremarkable.
Laboratory data were as follows: hemoglobin = 9.6 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) = 85 fL, white blood cells = 11,880/mm3 (neutrophil 81%, lymphocyte 4.9%, monocyte 12%), platelets = 384,000/mm3, international normalized ratio (INR) = 2.2, blood urea nitrogen = 73.8 mg/dL, serum creatinine = 7.78 mg/dL, sodium = 133 mEq/L, potassium = 3.8 mEq/L, bicarbonate = 16.8 mEq/L, chloride = 92 mEq/L, calcium = 8.7 mg/dL, phosphorus = 4.8 mg/dL, magnesium = 2.8 mg/dL, and glycated hemoglobin = 7.1%. Liver function tests revealed the following: total cholesterol = 177 mg/dL, total protein = 5.4 g/dL, albumin = 2.8 g/dL, globulin = 2.6 g/dL, total bilirubin = 36.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin = 35 mg/dL, alanine transaminase (ALT) = 53 U/L, aspartate transaminase (AST) = 109 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) = 408 U/L. Serum tests for human immunodeficiency virus, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were negative. Serum carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA 19-9) levels were 850 U/mL (0–37). Urine appeared greenish with a specific gravity of 1.010, trace proteinuria, 5–10 red blood cells (RBCs)/high-power field, 1–2 white blood cells/high-power field along with numerous muddy brown granular casts (Fig. ), and bile casts (Fig. ). No RBC casts were found. The fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) was 2.7%, and FEurea was 44.8%. Abdominal computed tomography showed 4.3 × 4.2 cm and 3.1 × 3.5 cm inhomogeneous enhancing masses at liver segment 4A with moderately dilated intrahepatic ducts (Fig. ). Based on the symptoms of biliary obstruction, typical imaging findings, and elevated serum CA 19-9, the most likely diagnosis was cholangiocarcinoma (Bismuth–Corlette type IIIb). He was initially given intravenous fluids to combat his oliguric renal failure, but there was no improvement in serum creatinine and urine output. Over the next 5 days, hemodialysis was initiated due to diuretic-resistant oliguric acute renal failure. Percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) was also performed to relieve his obstructive jaundice. However, he still experienced clinical deterioration. After approximately 2 weeks of hemodialysis, his total serum bilirubin continued to be in the 30 mg/dL range and he remained oliguric. On the 27th day of admission, the patient suffered from massive biliary hemorrhage requiring a blood transfusion. An emergency esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) was performed and revealed active hemorrhage from the ampulla of Vater, a finding suggestive of tumor bleeding. After thorough discussion and consultation between the patient's family and the palliative care team, the healthcare providers proceed to initiate palliative treatment.

Patient Note:
A 40-year-old African American male patient, known to have homozygous sickle cell disease (SS genotype) and associated nephropathy, was admitted to our medical service in the summer of 2020 with a painful hemolytic crisis. He had presented with several weeks of progressive generalized weakness, generalized myalgias, and worsening dyspnea in the previous week. He had failed to take his prophylactic hydroxyurea for some time prior to presentation. Initial evaluation in the emergency department (ED) revealed lethargy and sedated level of consciousness with hypoxia and increased work of breathing. Following initial resuscitative measures in the ED, he improved. Pertinent admission laboratory data included sickle hemoglobin (HbS) 94.5%, hemoglobin A2 3.2%, mean corpuscular volume 103 fL, reticulocyte count 19.4% (baseline reticulocyte count of ~2.8%), white blood cell count 34.85×109/L with 85% neutrophils, platelet count 294×109/L, hemoglobin 4.6 g/dL, and hematocrit 13.1%. Peripheral smear revealed sickled red blood cells (RBCs). Chemistry revealed sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 7.4 (3.5–5.0) mmol/L, glucose <20 mg/dL that quickly improved after 10% dextrose infusion, creatinine 7.20 mg/dL (baseline 1.5 mg/dL), bicarbonate 5 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, calcium 7.4 mg/dL, total bilirubin >40 mg/dL, phosphorus 11.6 mg/dL, conjugated bilirubin 38.7 mg/dL, unconjugated bilirubin 2.9 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 374 IU/L, alanine aminotransferase 94 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 418 IU/L, total protein 10.7 g/dL, albumin 3.6 g/dL, ferritin >6,000 ng/mL, creatine kinase 70 U/L, troponin I 0.042 (<0.034) ng/mL, ammonia 54 (<34) μmol/L, lactate dehydrogenase 3288 (313–618) U/L, N-terminal pro b-type natriuretic peptide 8940 (<125) pg/mL, partial thromboplastin time 31 s, prothrombin time 20.9 s, international normalized ratio 1.8, and lactic acid 11.6 mmol/L. The calculated MELD score was 40. Salicylate level was 1.7 mg/dL, and acetaminophen level was 14 μg/mL. Initial venous blood gas examination revealed pH 7.03, pCO2 38 mmHg, pO2 34 mmHg, TCO2 11 mmol/L, base deficit 19, and oxygen saturation was only 42%. Electrocardiogram (EKG) in the ED was abnormal and demonstrated sinus rhythm with prolonged QTc duration of 522 (<440) ms, together with inverted T waves in the lateral chest leads, V4–V6, consistent with true hyperkalemia (). He had promptly received emergency therapies for hyperkalemia including intravenous (IV) calcium gluconate infusion, IV sodium bicarbonate infusion, IV 10% dextrose infusion with insulin, and IV furosemide. In addition, he was started empirically on IV antibiotics, vancomycin, and meropenem.
He was transfused with two units of packed RBCs and was promptly transferred to the Medical ICU. He quickly underwent emergency RBC exchange with therapeutic apheresis via a right femoral vein cannula, utilizing 1,999 mL of packed RBCs with a net fluid balance of 270 mL. Sickle hemoglobin (HbS) quickly dropped to 26.3% post-apheresis exchange. Initial nephrology consultation on admission had recommended correction of metabolic acidosis and treatment of hyperkalemia with more isotonic sodium bicarbonate infusions, insulin/glucose infusions, and the initiation of oral sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (SZC). He received one hemodialysis treatment on day 3 following evidence of encephalopathy which was subsequently blamed on drug toxicity from IV ketamine that was used for pain management.
Despite observing significant improvement of the acute kidney injury on chronic kidney disease in the first two weeks of this admission with much improved serum creatinine 2–3 weeks into the admission, as well as normal urine output, and stabilization of serum bicarbonate level to 22–27 mmol/L, the patient had exhibited persistent hyperkalemia (5.2–6.7 mmol/L) even with continued administration of SZC at 10 mg 2 times daily. The simultaneous trajectories of serum creatinine, platelet count, and serum potassium concentrations during the admission are shown in . The patient had remained otherwise asymptomatic. Chlorthalidone, 25 mg daily, was added to manage the hyperkalemia without much success. Moreover, an EKG obtained in the 4th week of admission when the serum potassium that morning was 6.7 mmol/L was remarkably normal (). At this point, as demonstrated in , our patient had subsequently developed significantly progressive thrombocytosis, the result of previous treated sepsis. Admission leukocytosis of 34.85×109/L had decreased to 10.67×109/L in the 4th week of this admission. Nevertheless, usual therapies for hyperkalemia were again reinstituted in addition to the continued use of SZC and chlorthalidone.
From the foregoing observations and considerations, especially given the normal EKG shown in compared to the admission EKG in when he had true hyperkalemia with acute kidney injury and metabolic acidosis, for the very first time since this admission the possibility of pseudohyperkalemia was entertained. Consequently, later that same afternoon, following a conference call between the Nephrology and Internal Medicine Services, we repeated the simultaneous serum and plasma potassium measurements (after the emergency therapies for true hyperkalemia had been administered earlier that morning for a measured serum potassium level of 6.7 mmol/L). The measured simultaneous blood potassium levels were 5.8 mmol/L in serum versus 5.2 mmol/L in plasma. The reference range for blood potassium is 3.5–5.0 mmol/L. This confirmed the presence of pseudohyperkalemia secondary to the prevailing progressive thrombocytosis during this later part of the admission (). Pseudohyperkalemia is diagnosed when the serum potassium concentration exceeds that of plasma by at least 0.4 mmol/L. Subsequently, the new potassium binder SZC was discontinued.
Five weeks after discharge, the patient was re-admitted to the same medical service this time with symptomatic painful sickle cell anemia crisis, fevers, cough with green sputum production, leukocytosis of 31.98×109/L, bilirubin 8.4 mg/dL, and chest radiograph that demonstrated bilateral multifocal patchy infiltrates consistent with pneumonia. Both HIV 1 and 2 and repeated COVID-19 RT-PCR tests were negative. He was started empirically on IV ceftriaxone, IV azithromycin, and IV vancomycin. Blood cultures grew methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus within 15 hours in two out of two bottles, and IV antibiotics were de-escalated to 6 weeks of IV vancomycin administration. Admission serum creatinine was only slightly increased at 1.48 mg/dL, potassium was 5.7 mmol/L, but bicarbonate was 23 mmol/L. The patient responded well to IV antibiotics with resolution of the fever, and WBC had quickly dropped to 14.99×109/L after 2 days. Despite this quick overall clinical improvement, persistent hyperkalemia was again recorded throughout this second admission despite a low-potassium diet, IV furosemide, and the addition of oral fludrocortisone, 0.2 mg daily. Admission platelet count of 546 ×109/L had decreased to 314–450×109/L following IV fluids and IV antibiotics. This time round, simultaneous serum and plasma potassium levels were checked multiple times, and serum and plasma potassium concentrations were similar, indicative of true hyperkalemia. Hyperkalemia in the past had been related to pseudohyperkalemia associated with thrombocytosis. Factors contributory to true hyperkalemia were suspected to include sepsis accompanying the sickle cell crisis and intravascular hemolysis, concurrent exposure to heparin (subcutaneous enoxaparin 40 mg daily for deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis), and underlying sickle cell nephropathy with chronic kidney disease. The patient was restarted on SZC 10 g b.i.d. for 48 hours, and continued at 10–15 g daily. Serum potassium was maintained in the 5.3–6.3 mmol/L range throughout the hospital stay. The administration of SZC was to be continued post-discharge for 6 weeks with monitoring of serum potassium levels. To our knowledge, the patient has remained stable since discharge from the hospital.

Patient Note:
A now four-year-old boy presented at the age of 3 years to our emergency department with a 2-week history of fever (39.5 °C), vomiting, and watery stools, which had already improved. He also had lower limb oedema and periorbital puffiness for one week. Apart from receiving paracetamol for the fever, there was no history of medication use. He is the offspring of parents in a consanguineous marriage with an unremarkable past medical history. He has one sister and three brothers. One of his brothers was diagnosed with steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome due to membranous proliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) with C3 deposition diagnosed by kidney biopsy at 3 years of age. He reached chronic kidney disease stage 5 at the age of 4 years. Dialysis was considered, but he died in another hospital due to sepsis. No genetic testing was done on his brother.
On initial examination, the patient was stable but hypertensive with blood pressure reading of 136/83 mmHg, generalized oedema and a distended abdomen. No other abnormalities detected in systemic examinations. Laboratory investigations were performed and revealed the following: serum creatinine (45 µmol/L; normal 2.6–52.2 µmol/L), blood urea nitrogen (14.1 mmol/L; normal 1.8–6.4 mmol/L), potassium (5.5 mmol/L), sodium (135 mmol/L), chloride (110 mmol/L), bicarbonate (18 mmol/L), albumin (1.2 g/dL; normal 3.4-4.2 g/dL), haemoglobin (Hgb) (5.2 g/dL; normal 11.5–14.5 g/dL), white blood cell (WBC) count (9.13 × 109/L; normal 4–12 × 109/L), platelet count (23 × 109/L; normal 150–400 × 109/L), haptoglobin (< 0.058 g/L; normal 0.5–2.2 g/L), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) (1425 U/L; normal 150–500 U/L), complement C3 (C3) (0.550 g/L; normal 0.80–1.60 g/L), and complement C4 (C4) (0.109/L; normal 0.16–0.48 g/L). The patient also had normal coagulation profile, normal level of ADAMTS13, negative coombs test and peripheral blood smears showed schistocytes. His urine dipstick showed urine protein excretion of > 400 mg/dL and 20–50 red blood cells/high power field. Stool cultures and analysis revealed no pathogens (Table ). Due to his uncontrolled hypertension and thrombocytopenia, the patient could not undergo a kidney biopsy.
The patient’s clinical picture fit the diagnosis of aHUS, and eculizumab was started within 24 h of the diagnosis. Following the recommendations of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the manufacturer, he was given a 600 mg intravenous (IV) infusion as induction therapy and a 300 mg IV infusion every two weeks as maintenance therapy. After two doses of eculizumab, he started to show clinical and laboratory improvement. His Hgb improved to 9 g/dL; his platelet count increased to 169 × 109/L; and his C3, haptoglobin, and LDH levels were normalized (Table ). His blood pressure was controlled on amlodipine and lisinopril, and his oedema significantly improved. A genetic panel for aHUS was performed, and the patient showed a homozygous nonsense gene mutation in DGKE p.(Phe250Serfs*3). No mutation was detected in the gene coding regions of ADAMTS13, C3, CD46, CFB, CFH, CFHR1, CFHR2, CFHR3, CFHR5, CFI, MMACHC, PIGA, PLG, THBD, CD59, CR1, CR2, INF2, or MUT.
Six months after presentation, the patient was stable and in clinical remission on eculizumab 300 mg every other week. His laboratory parameters were within the normal ranges (Hgb 11.8 g/dL, platelet count 543 × 109/L, C3 0.9 g/L) apart from persistent moderate proteinuria, with urine dipstick 100 mg/dL and serum albumin 2.5 g/dL (Table ). Based on the genetic results and the patient’s general condition, we decided to start increasing the time between eculizumab doses, targeting discontinuation. A 300 mg IV infusion of eculizumab was administered every three weeks instead of every two weeks. Following the first dose after increasing the time between doses, the patient was noticed to be oedematous; his proteinuria in urine dipstick increased to 400 − 300 mg/dL with urine protein/creatinine ratio > 3 mg/mg, his albumin dropped to 1.9 g/dL, and other laboratory parameters, including Hgb and platelet count, were within the normal ranges (Hgb 12.2 g/dl and platelet count 494 × 109/L) (Table ). His oedema improved after the addition of oral furosemide at 1 mg/kg/dose twice daily. Nevertheless, after 4 months of eculizumab every 3 weeks, the patient’s proteinuria and albumin levels did not improve. The decision was made to try eculizumab every two weeks and observe his proteinuria. Interestingly, his proteinuria improved to 100 mg/dL after only two doses of the two-week regimen, his oedema subsided and serum albumin improved gradually from 1.9 g/L to 2.2 g/L (Table ). Four months later, patient is stable on eculizumab 300 mg IV infusion every two weeks with no oedema and stable proteinuria and Albumin (Table ). Throughout his first year after presentation, apart from proteinuria, his creatinine was within the normal range, and he had no signs of HUS activity after the first remission.

Patient Note:
A 47-year-old African American man came to the emergency department with complaints of 4 days of severe, colicky back pain with some radiation to the flanks, associated with nausea and vomiting and a noticeable decrease in urination. He denied any history of trauma, stones, dysuria, frequency, hesitancy, rash, constipation, diarrhea, fever, or previous similar pain. He denied any unusual food or sick contact. He denied taking any medications for the past 18 months, including nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), acetaminophen, and antacids. He denied smoking, alcohol, and drugs although it had been noted in the chart that he had a positive urinary toxicology screen in the past for cocaine. Although he had a history of hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia diagnosed 3 years previously during a brief hospital stay, he was not taking any of the recommended prescription medications. Family history was positive for premature atherosclerosis, yet negative for autoimmune diseases and tuberculosis. On physical examination, his vital signs included blood pressure of 235/125 mm Hg, pulse 90 beats/min, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 37°C, O2 saturation 96% on room air. He was awake and alert, had clear lungs, a regular heart rate with no murmurs, gallops, or rubs, a soft abdomen with normal bowels sounds, bilateral costophrenic tenderness to percussion, a normal prostate, and neurologic examination.
Creatinine was 10.48 mg/dL, up from his previous level of 1.15 mg/dL at his previous visit 3 years prior. Blood urea nitrogen 63 mg/dL, Na 134 mEq/L, K 4.4 mEq/L, Cl 100 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, glucose 151 mg/dL, Ca 9.4 mg/dL, PO4 6.1 mg/dL, Mg 2.1 mg/dL, hemoglobin 15 g/dL, hematocrit 45%, white blood cells (WBC) of 9100 with neutrophils 75%, lymphocytes 12.5%, and eosinophils 0.9%, platelets of 167 000. His troponin was 0.02 ng/mL, CK-MB 2.6 ng/mL, BNP 53 pg/mL, amylase 70 U/L, lipase 69 U/L, international normalized ratio 1.1, prothrombin time 13 seconds, partial thromboplastin time 31 seconds, HbA1c 6.6%, aspartate transaminotransferase (AST) 25 IU/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 20 IU/L, albumin 3.8 g/dL, total protein 7.3 g/dL, bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 39 IU/L, C-reactive protein 25 mg/L. Urine toxicology screen was positive for cocaine. Urinalysis showed 2+ blood, no red blood cells, no WBCs, including no eosinophils by special stain, no casts, no albumin, negative for nitrates, negative leukocyte esterase and negative for bacteria, trace ketone, pH 5.5, and specific gravity 1.015. No pigmented granular casts. His hepatitis panel: C-ANCA (cytoplasmic–antineutrophil cytoplasm antibodies) Ag, P-ANCA (perinuclear–antineutrophil cytoplasm antibodies), anti–glomerular basement membrane Ag, anti-myeloperoxidase, antinuclear antibody and Helicobacter pylori IgA were all negative. Creatine phosphokinase was 127 mg/dL and his serum electrophoresis was normal. C3 was 119 mg/dL and C4 was 88.5 mg/dL (both normal). Anti-proteinase 3 (PR-3) antibodies were positive.
His chest and abdominal radiographs were normal, and computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast showed no urinary obstruction. Transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal wall motion and ejection fraction. Electrocardiography showed nonspecific T-wave abnormalities, which were unchanged since 2010.
The patient’s blood pressure was initially controlled with intravenous labetalol and hydralazine. Over the first 3 days of admission, his urinary output decreased from 700 cm3/d to less than 400 cm3/d and his creatinine rose to 13.6 mg/dL. Nephrology was consulted early and after ruling out acute toxic nephropathy, urinary obstruction, and acute rhabdomyolsis, it was suspected, based on his initial positive toxicology screen for cocaine that he may have cocaine-induced interstitial nephritis and a renal biopsy was performed (see below). Indeed, on further questioning, the patient admitted to smoking crack cocaine. It was confirmed with the patient and his spouse that the cocaine was not mixed or combined with any other substance. At that point (day 4) the patient was started on intravenous methylprednisolone at 125 mg every 6 hours as well as daily or every other day hemodialysis for a total of 6 sessions. The methylprednisolone was tapered to 80 mg every 6 hours after 3 days and then changed to oral prednisone, which was furthered tapered over the next 12 days. The steroid was used roughly day 4 of admission, and 7 days after symptoms had started, it is hard to tag a response to the steroid separately as the patient was started on both hemodialysis and steroid at the same time after biopsy of kidney confirmed the diagnosis; nevertheless, we were able to see stable improvement on creatinine on days 8 and 9, with improvement of urine output (roughly 1000-1500 cm3/d). The patient stabilized with his creatinine at 3.54 mg/dL before being discharged home and follow-up 8 weeks later showed a normal creatinine.
Renal biopsy () showed normocellular glomeruli, interstitial atrophy, and fibrosis with tubular loss of 10% to 20%. There were foci of interstitial inflammation composed of lymphocytes, plasma cells, eosinophils, and edema with areas of severe arterioarterosclerosis. No immune complexes were detected.

Patient Note:
A 56-year-old male was brought to the emergency department by paramedics for a syncopal episode and inability to move his lower extremities. He complained of abdominal pain and inability to move his legs beginning five hours prior to arrival. He awakened from a nap that afternoon and experienced numbness in both legs, which progressed to paralysis. At baseline, he was ambulatory without any history of weakness and was last ambulatory hours prior. He endorsed occasional pain in his legs when walking at baseline.
He also complained of abdominal pain with nausea and vomiting for two days. He had a history of alcohol use and reported dark-colored emesis and last bowel movement three days prior. On review of symptoms the patient denied any history of headache, dizziness, chest pain, back pain, trauma, fevers, or extremity weakness. He walked longer than a mile the day prior. History from the patient’s wife revealed that he had an episode of altered level of consciousness while on the couch and that finding combined with his abdominal pain and paralysis prompted her to call 911. The patient’s past medical history was significant for hypertension, peripheral arterial disease, and myocardial infarction 10 years prior. His past surgical history included “abdominal stents” and a left carotid stent. His social history was significant for a 25-pack/year history of smoking and daily alcohol. His medications included atenolol and ranitidine.
Physical exam showed an oral temperature of 36.7°C, blood pressure 107/65mmHg, heart rate 99 beats/minute and a respiratory rate of 30 breaths/min, with oxygen saturation on 15L non-rebreather mask of 94%. His weight was 72.5kg and he appeared older than his stated age. He was alert, cooperative and in moderate distress, primarily complaining of pain and cramping in his lower extremities and repeatedly asking staff to straighten out his legs although they were already lying straight and motionless on the gurney. His head exam was unremarkable with the exception of a dry oropharynx. Cervical spine, cardiac, and lung exams were unremarkable. His abdomen was firm and diffusely tender to palpation with generalized rebound and guarding. An irreducible left inguinal hernia was present. He had vomiting, and placement of a nasogastric tube revealed 1.5 liters of coffee-ground emesis. Lower extremities were thin, cool, and without any palpable or Dopplerable pulses in bilateral femoral, popliteal or pedal distribution. There was trace non-reproducible sensation to the mottled lower extremities, and no sensation distal to the ankles. Motor exam was significant for lower extremity paralysis.
Laboratory data consisted of sodium 121mEq/L (135–145), potassium 6.8mEq/L (3.3–4.8), chloride 89mEq/L (101–111), bicarbonate 18mEq/L (25–34), BUN 31mg/dL (8–26), creatinine 1.5mg/dL (0.5–1.3), and blood glucose 367mg/dL (70–115). White blood cell 19.1thous/mcL (4.0–10.5), hemoglobin 12.8g/dL (13.5–16.9), lipase 107U/L (22–51), hematocrit 38.8% (39.5–50.0), and platelets 165thous/mcL (150–400). There was a left shift in the neutrophils 16.2thous/mcL (85%) (2.0–8.1). Alkaline phosphate 88IU/L (26–110), AST 84IU/L (8–40), ALT 38IU/L (0.0–60), total bilirubin 1mg/dL (0.0–1.4), total protein 5.4g/dL (6.1–8.2), albumin 2.6 g/dL (3.2–5.5). Lactate 7.2mmol/L (0.7–2.1). ABG showed pH of 7.25 (7.38–7.42), pCO2 31.6mmHg (36–42), pO2 123.6mmHg (80–104), bicarbonate 13.5mmol/L (21–27). PT was 17.3sec (9.5–12.3), PTT 45.9sec (24.1–35.1), and INR 1.62 (0.87–1.14). B-type natriuretic peptide 1,950pg/mL (<100). Troponin 2.75ng/mL (<0.03).
Chest radiograph was unremarkable. Electrocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus rhythm at 95 beats/minute with ST elevation inferiorly, anteriorly and laterally (). Bedside ultrasound to evaluate the abdominal aorta was limited. Vascular surgery was consulted prior to CT for concern of a vascular catastrophe. Cardiology was consulted for the patient’s ECG findings consistent with myocardial infarction. The patient went for a non-contrast head CT that was unremarkable and a CT angiogram of the chest, abdomen and pelvis, which was significant for the abdominal aorta with no contrast opacification 2.2cm superior to the bifurcation (), high-grade stenosis of the right common iliac artery, complete occlusion of the left common iliac artery, stents in the celiac artery and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), evidence of occlusion of the proximal SMA and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), hepatic, splenic, bilateral renal infarctions, left inguinal hernia, bowel obstruction, pneumatosis intestinalis with evidence of ischemic bowel, and aspiration in the right lower lung. General surgery was consulted.
The cardiology service stated that the patient was not a candidate for cardiac catheterization and to start anticoagulation and low-dose aspirin if there was no contraindication or planned surgery. They also recommended thrombolytics for the diffuse thrombotic disease. The patient did have an echocardiography study that revealed an ejection fraction of 35% and multiple regional wall motion abnormalities. General surgery recommended comfort measures as he was not a surgical candidate. Vascular surgery commented that bypass would be futile and if patient survived, would be a candidate for extra-anatomical axillo-bifemoral bypass in the future. The patient was admitted to the medical intensive care unit for broad-spectrum antibiotics and a heparin drip and succumbed to his illness the following day.

Patient Note:
A 68-year-old woman with a past medical history of metastatic melanoma, hypertension, deep vein thrombosis, and pulmonary embolism with inferior vena cava filter placement presented with altered mental status, hypotension, and fever. She had received her second dose of immunotherapy with ipilimumab+nivolumab shortly before presentation.
Initial vitals were significant for blood pressure of 75/40 mm Hg, heart rate of 110 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 95% on room air, and temperature 98.4 F. Physical exam was remarkable for altered mental status, moderate respiratory distress, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Initial blood work before fluid resuscitation showed Hg 10.3 g/dL; WBC 13.9 K/mcL; Platelets 164 K/mcL; Na 133 mEq/L; K 3.1 mEq/L; Cl 110 mEq/L; HCO3− 10 mEq/L; BUN of 23 mg/dL; Cr 3.2 mg/dL; Plasma Glucose 103 mg/dL; Total Protein 5.0 g/dL; Albumin 2.6 g/dL; Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST) 737 U/L; Alanine Aminotransferase (ALA) 399 U/L; Alkaline Phosphatase (ALK) 99 U/L; Total Bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL; Direct Bilirubin 0.4 mg/dL; Calcium 7.3 mg/dL; Magnesium1.5 mg/dL; Inorganic Phosphorus 2.5 mg/dL; Procalcitonin 59.11 ng/mL; Lactic Acid 1.7 mmol/L; PT 17.7 SEC/INR 1.46; aPTT 49.2 SEC; COVID-19 PCR negative; SARS-CoV-2-IgG negative; Influenza Type A negative; Influenza Type B negative; Venous blood gas: pH 7.24, pCO2 of 24 mmHg ().
Additional diagnostic evaluations included the following:Electrocardiogram: normal sinus rhythm with premature ventricular contractions, heart rate of 94 beats per minute CXR: no focal consolidation or pleural effusion Computed tomography abdomen/pelvis w/o contrast: perinephric stranding and cholestasis Computed tomography head w/o contrast: no new acute intracranial abnormality or mass effect UA w/electrolytes: few white blood cells and red blood cells but no casts, urinary pH of 7.5, urine anion gap of 40, and fractional excretion of sodium of 0.6%
The patient underwent prophylactic right femur nailing with excision of a metastatic lesion and developed a fever of unknown origin, hypotension, acute blood loss, and acute tubular necrosis two weeks before presentation. At that time, she completed treatment with vancomycin, aztreonam, and metronidazole due to a penicillin allergy. However, the infectious workup remained negative throughout the admission. The patient remained afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and safely discharged to a subacute rehabilitation facility.
Four days after discharge, she presented to our emergency department for fever and hypotension. The team initiated the sepsis protocol, and the patient was adequately fluid resuscitated with an appropriate blood pressure response. Blood cultures were collected, and the patient received empiric antibiotic treatment and stress dose hydrocortisone with improvement in her mental status.
Throughout the hospital course, her functional status improved significantly. The patient again agreed to placement in a subacute rehab facility on day fourteen of hospitalization.
The clinical picture was suggestive of sepsis with multiorgan failure in the setting of a new RTA. The patient continued on antibiotics, maintenance fluids with lactated ringers, started on a sodium bicarbonate drip, and was admitted to the medical oncology service for further management. Working differential diagnoses included sepsis, adrenal insufficiency, antimicrobial toxicity, and immunotherapy adverse effects.
The patient completed a seven-day course of antibiotics. Infectious workup, including computed tomography abdominal and pelvis w/o contrast, urine culture, blood cultures, chest x-ray, and transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE), was unremarkable. While tapering the stress dose hydrocortisone, the patient developed transient hypotension, fever, and transaminitis episodes. Per discussion with the infectious disease team and primary oncologist, the thought was that the clinical presentation was likely related to immunotherapy.
Despite the acute renal failure resolution, the patient remained with a severe hyperchloremic non-anion gap metabolic acidosis, along with persistent hypokalemia (the nadir of 2.4 mEq/L) indicating a distal (type-I) RTA.
Although possible, antimicrobial-induced RTA was unlikely due to the resolution of the renal failure and that none of the agents are known culprits to manifest acid/base disturbances []. Giving rise to a suspected immunotherapy-induced RTA, the patient was started on prednisone one mg/kg and transitioned to sodium bicarbonate tablets. Repeat blood work revealed an appropriate response with downtrending liver enzymes and increased serum sodium bicarbonate, indicating a resolving RTA.
Further workup revealed a negative autoimmune panel (anti-Ro (SS-A), anti-La (SS-B), ANA). SPEP, UPEP, free light chains, hepatitis serology, and thyroid function tests were unremarkable. Alkaline urine, a positive urinary anion gap, a nonanion gap metabolic acidosis with low serum bicarbonate, and persistent hypokalemia suggested a distal-type-1 RTA.
The patient followed up with nephrology and oncology as an outpatient. She completed a prednisone taper over four weeks and denied symptoms at the follow-up encounter.

Patient Note:
A 72-year-old male arrived at the hospital complaining of a two-day history of productive cough associated with moderate shortness of breath and a new onset of chills. He notes an intermittent low-grade fever that began the previous morning but reports that it has been steadily climbing, culminating in a measured temperature of 38.2°C on presentation. For the past week, he has also experienced increasing fatigue and mild confusion upon exertion, though he remains oriented to person, place, and time. He describes a background of heart-related issues that have previously resulted in occasional fluid accumulation in his lower extremities and frequent monitoring of his cardiovascular status. Despite these concerns, he has remained relatively stable at home and was able to carry on daily activities without sanctioned assistance of any formal institutional care. However, he does mention that physical tasks around the house, such as walking upstairs or carrying groceries, have been increasingly challenging over the last month. He admits to having chronic kidney-related problems that require periodic assessment of his renal function; these problems sometimes manifest as difficulty maintaining stable metabolic balances, especially during episodes of reduced oral intake or additional physical stress. Upon examination, his vital signs show a heart rate of 96 beats per minute, a respiratory rate of 24 breaths per minute, a systolic blood pressure of 128 mm Hg, and a notable elevation in temperature consistent with his complaint of fever. Room air measurements suggest that his oxygen concentration is somewhat lower than ideal, with a partial pressure level in his arterial blood of 78 mm Hg that indicates the need for close observation and potential supplemental support. His serum studies reveal a blood urea measurement above the typical reference range, measured at 32 mg/dL, a level that would be concerning in the context of his chronic organ dysfunction, though this finding is not unfamiliar in light of his longstanding kidney issues. Laboratory analysis of his blood chemistry shows a perfectly acceptable electrolyte balance for certain key parameters, with a sodium level of 140 mEq/L, though the clinical team remains alert to any shifts that might occur if his condition were to worsen. His hematocrit is measured at 37%, which is on the lower end of normal but does not, in isolation, point to any acute hematologic disorder. His glucose level of 120 mg/dL seems unremarkable, especially given the mild physiologic stress he is under from possible infection. Additional testing includes an evaluation of his acid-base status, with a pH of 7.42, which is near normal at the time of assessment, suggesting there is no overt metabolic or respiratory derangement beyond what one might expect in a moderate respiratory infection. He denies any previous episodes of sudden limb weakness, speech disturbance, or other neurological deficits that would raise suspicion of a major vascular event in his brain. Chest imaging performed in the emergency setting shows inflammatory infiltrates consistent with an infectious process, but no clear evidence of fluid collection in the pleural space. He lives on his own with fairly steady day-to-day support from family, having adjusted to a slower pace of activity over the years. Although he has some preexisting medical conditions, he has, until this week, managed them with routine visits to his outpatient providers and has not had a recent hospitalization. He now appears acutely ill and requires further evaluation for a likely pulmonary infection, with close monitoring of both his cardiovascular and kidney status to guard against any complications arising from treatment or disease progression.

Patient Note:
A 65-year-old female presented to the emergency department complaining of several days of progressively worsening cough, intermittent chills, and mild difficulty breathing. She noted that her cough became more persistent over the past 48 hours with occasional production of light yellow sputum. At home, she recorded a low-grade fever and used over-the-counter fever reducers without significant relief. On examination, she appeared slightly tachypneic, with a measured respiratory rate of 22 breaths per minute. Her pulse was recorded at 110 beats per minute, and her blood pressure was 115 mm Hg systolic on arrival. She admitted to experiencing increased fatigue and some generalized malaise. A temperature of 100.1 degrees Fahrenheit was documented, and auscultation of the chest revealed crackles in the right lower lung field consistent with an infectious process. She had dealt with a significant cardiac issue in the past, which required medical management to prevent fluid buildup in her lungs and peripheral tissues, and she continued to follow a regimen to mitigate these episodes. She also disclosed a remote neurological event that led to a period of rehabilitation, although she had largely regained her usual level of mobility afterward. She denied any chronic problems that would suggest persistent kidney dysfunction, outlining that her laboratory tests had historically been stable. She was unaware of any concerning conditions characterized by abnormal cell growth, stating that she had never received such a diagnosis. Laboratory findings at this presentation showed a sodium level of 133 mEq/L, which was slightly below normal, and a blood urea nitrogen measurement of 27 mg/dL. Her serum glucose was measured at 135 mg/dL, and hematocrit was recorded at 38%. An arterial blood gas test revealed a pH of 7.36, and the partial pressure of oxygen measured 82 mm Hg, indicating some degree of respiratory compromise but without the need for immediate assisted ventilation. A chest radiograph demonstrated a focal opacity in the right lower zone strongly suggestive of pneumonia, and there was no radiographic indication of a significant fluid collection within the pleural space. She reported no recent significant changes in exercise tolerance, although she had chosen to rest more often than usual in the past week due to her cough and low stamina. She mentioned occasional dizziness, which she attributed to feeling under the weather rather than any chronic underlying issue. Physical examination revealed unremarkable findings apart from the noted right-sided lower lung findings and mild tachycardia. Her skin was warm, and peripheral perfusion remained adequate. The patient’s past experiences with episodes of fluid retention and her previous neurological recovery shape her current risk profile, especially in the context of an acute infection. Her symptoms arose a few days prior to presentation, and she initially tried over-the-counter treatments, which failed to alleviate her feverish sensation and shortness of breath. She did not mention any new exposures to sick contacts outside of her household and denied recent travel. She was fully conscious and oriented, conversing well despite short bursts of cough. According to her account, she had not experienced changes in bowel or urinary habits. She seemed somewhat concerned about her progressive breathing symptoms, prompting her to seek urgent evaluation. Overall, her initial presentation was suggestive of a straightforward infectious process, but clinicians took into account her documented background of cardiac and neurological vulnerability when assessing her overall condition, particularly given her borderline low sodium and elevated heart rate. All findings were documented prior to any acute interventions or aggressive management in the emergency department, and her vital signs and laboratory data pointed toward an infectious etiology that required prompt diagnostic and therapeutic measures.

Patient Note:
A 68-year-old male presented to the emergency department after three days of progressive shortness of breath, productive cough, and generalized malaise. He noted an intermittent fever at home, which worsened on the day of admission, along with chills and night sweats. He has been feeling more fatigued than usual, struggling to complete his routine morning walks without pausing to catch his breath, and has also experienced mild chest tightness upon exertion. On initial evaluation, he was awake, alert, and oriented, conversing clearly without lapses in memory or concentration. He denied experiencing any acute episodes of unilateral weakness, facial droop, or slurred speech in the past, and he reported no longstanding liver problems. According to him, fluid overload symptoms such as progressive leg swelling, difficulty breathing while lying flat, or episodes of nighttime coughing have not been issues. He described a past difficulty with kidney function, requiring follow-up with specialists, and also revealed a remotest history of a malignant growth for which he received treatment several years ago. On further questioning, he stated that his usual medications include those needed to monitor both his chronic conditions and his past oncologic management, although he did not recall any medication specifically for significantly compromised cardiac output. He claimed that he has never been told he suffered from sudden neurologic attacks or blood supply problems to his brain and had not visited any facility for such concerns. The patient had a documented pulse oximetry reading that correlated with her observed shortness of breath, prompting concern for community-onset pulmonary infection. When asked about the current day or location, she provided statements that were confusing or unrelated, such as referencing an unrelated setting she believed she had just visited earlier, which conflicted with the eyewitness account that she had been parked at that convenience store for well over an hour. Her mental status appeared muddled, as she repeated some questions back and then trailed off into silence, giving no firm grasp of the reality of the situation. Despite this perplexed speech pattern, when explicitly directed to perform simple motor tasks, she demonstrated an ability to comply with specific requests. These actions indicated that her strength and coordination were at least somewhat intact and that she was able to comprehend spoken commands on a rudimentary level. However, she continued to keep her eyes firmly shut through most of the examination, remaining passive and largely still, except when asked to move or shift as part of the clinician’s assessment. Her past medical records, retrieved from an old hospital card located in her wallet, mentioned a prior hospitalization several years ago for an ischemic stroke that allegedly left her with mild residual weakness in her left leg, although there was no documentation of any severe long-term mobility limitations. It was unclear whether she had been adhering to that medication regimen in recent months. Family members, who were reached by telephone shortly after her arrival, mentioned that she had occasionally reported episodes of dizziness and general fatigue, but they had not noticed any acute changes in her mental status until just a few days prior, when she started to appear withdrawn and less interactive during phone calls. She reportedly had some difficulty recalling recent conversations, although she was not formally evaluated for cognitive decline. No significant cuts or bruises were found on her head or neck, and she did not complain of headache when queried. A more comprehensive workup was launched, including blood tests to check for renal function imbalances or possible infectious processes, imaging studies to rule out a new cerebrovascular event, and a toxicological screen to exclude possible external contributors. A partial pressure of oxygen around 75 mm Hg was documented while she received a relatively low level of supplemental oxygen (FiO2 at 25%), suggesting that her respiratory system was maintaining adequate gas exchange for the moment. Nevertheless, close monitoring was deemed essential given her clinical presentation. In terms of laboratory results, she was found to have a sodium concentration of 135 mEq/L and a potassium concentration of 3.8 mEq/L, both of which fell within reasonable parameters, although attention to potassium was noted given the interplay of renal function and cardiac rhythm. Notably, her creatinine level was 1.9 mg/dL, which stood out as an abrupt departure from her previously normal evaluations, raising the concern that her kidney function had recently deteriorated over a short period. No evidence had been uncovered of ongoing long-term disorders that might have predisposed her to significant immune compromise, and her family could not recall any prior episodes suggestive of major organ dysfunction. At the same time, her white blood cell count hovered at 12,000 per cubic millimeter, mildly elevated but not exceedingly high, which could indicate an early inflammatory or infectious process. Her Glasgow Coma Scale score was calculated to be 8 on initial assessment. A rapid screen of her medical history did not uncover any previous mention of unremitting kidney ailments or debilitating immune conditions. Family members confirmed she had not been on long-term immunosuppressive therapies and that her overall health had been reasonable up until the past week. The ICU team noted that her mild elevation in heart rate, moderate elevation in respiratory rate, and borderline blood pressures could reflect the body’s stress response to an unrecognized acute process. She was not placed on any intravenous fluids or medications before these measurements, ensuring that the collected data represented her baseline condition at the time of presentation. Given the worrisome drop in her mental status, the new rise in creatinine, and the possibility of an emerging infection or another stressor, the decision was made to observe her closely in a high-acuity setting. Plans were formed to evaluate further for any respiratory compromise, watch her urine output meticulously, and repeat imaging alongside laboratory work in an effort to pinpoint the cause of her sudden deterioration. As her baseline medical profile did not suggest extensive previous organ disease or a compromised immune state, the team focused on acute triggers that might have precipitated this severe change. If any covert concerns such as occult infections or other organ complications were uncovered, immediate targeted management would be initiated. A 57-year-old male was brought to the intensive care unit by emergency medical transport after family members noted that he had been experiencing high fever, persistent fatigue, and progressive weakness over the preceding several days. At the time of admission, his skin was warm, and his measured temperature was recorded at approximately 100.2 degrees Fahrenheit. He appeared somewhat fatigued but was alert and oriented, demonstrating normal eye-opening, motor commands, and verbal responses that together indicated an unimpaired overall level of consciousness (Glasgow Coma Score: 15). Monitoring of his hemodynamics showed a systolic pressure of 116 mm Hg with a diastolic reading of 57 mm Hg. His heart rate was measured at 85 beats per minute, and he manifested an elevated respiratory frequency of 28 breaths per minute, suggestive of labored breathing or an increased demand for ventilation. Arterial blood sampling revealed a slightly acidic trend in the bloodstream, with values near 7.30. Laboratory investigations also highlighted that his serum sodium level was 150 mEq/L—a clear indication of hypernatremia—and his potassium level was 5.1 mEq/L, which placed him at the upper margin of typical reference intervals. During the initial evaluation, concerns relating to his kidney function became a pressing focus, as he was known to have a baseline history of significantly compromised renal status that had been documented in prior clinical visits. More recently, he had shown signs of a further downturn in renal excretory capacity, and blood testing uncovered a creatinine concentration of 3.0 mg/dL at presentation, well above normal thresholds. This finding was accompanied by historical notes suggesting that his kidney function had been chronically diminished for a prolonged duration, yet had suffered a more acute drop. He reported a shortened urine output over the last two days, raising the possibility of superimposed kidney injury on a longstanding deficit in glomerular filtration. Hematologic parameters were notable—his red cell volume fraction rested at roughly 44%, indicating relatively stable oxygen-carrying capacity, and his white blood cell concentration was approximately 13,000 per cubic millimeter, a mild elevation that could point toward inflammation or infection. On review of his respiratory data, it emerged that he was receiving a moderate additional supply of inhaled oxygen that amounted to somewhat more than room air but remained well below half of the total inspired mixture, specifically an inspired oxygen fraction (FiO2) of approximately 38%. Even with this supplemental assistance, the partial pressure of oxygen recorded in arterial testing was 72 mm Hg, consistent with a mild decrement in how effectively his lungs were transferring oxygen. From a systemic standpoint, he described generalized malaise and occasional dizziness, but the most prominent symptoms were his feverish state, newfound breathing difficulty, and concerns about potential shifts in mental clarity, although at the precise moment of arrival his mental status still allowed full interactive communication. In discussing his medical background, he recounted years of intermittent consultations for persistent kidney problems—characterized by suboptimal filtration indices well beyond mild elevations—yet had not undergone any major surgical procedures or invasive interventions during this admission. He also mentioned multiple episodes in the past when his kidney function briefly worsened after acute illnesses or disruptions in his fluid balance. Given that his test results revealed a concerning level of creatinine, that he had a moderate rise in temperature, and that his breathing rate was distinctly elevated, the admitting team worried about the threat of compounding metabolic derangements, possible volume overload, or accumulation of unexcreted waste products. Although his mental responsiveness remained normal for the moment, the rapid nature of his deteriorating kidney performance in conjunction with systemic signs of stress merited close observation. Clinicians also noted the elevated potassium value, which could become more problematic if his renal status continued to erode. Additional imaging studies and more thorough investigations were arranged forthwith to check for structural problems in his urinary outflow tract or occult sources of infection that might aggravate his fragile physiology. During this initial phase, before introducing intravenous fluids, medications, or renal support, the team closely tracked his hemodynamic numbers and biochemical markers, prompting discussions about the need for urgent measures should his overall condition worsen. The hope was that rapid identification and management of any superimposed causes might help stabilize both his breathing pattern and his deteriorating excretory function.

Patient Note:
A 61-year-old male arrived in the intensive care setting with reports from those close to him that he had been unusually fatigued, breathing heavily, and showing signs of worsening confusion for roughly 24 hours leading up to admission. He has long been known to have a compromised internal defense mechanism, which places him at risk for more significant complications when even minor health issues arise. On examination at the time of arrival, his body temperature registered at 99.8 degrees Fahrenheit, a little under triple digits in Fahrenheit, indicating a mild elevation rather than a high-grade spike. He appeared quite tachypneic, taking 30 breaths per minute, and his heart rate was also elevated at 115 beats per minute, suggesting his body was under considerable stress. His blood pressure readings showed a systolic measurement of 121 mm Hg and a diastolic of 75 mm Hg. Initial laboratory tests indicated that his serum sodium was 131 mEq/L, on the lower end of normal for most healthy adults, while his serum potassium was 5.2 mEq/L, slightly above what many would consider the upper threshold of normal. Of even greater concern was his significantly elevated measurement reflecting kidney filtration efficiency, with a creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL, which came back much higher than hoped, confirming that he has both a baseline impairment in excretory function and a sudden worsening of that deficit at present. This was consistent with his clinical story of having had trouble maintaining stable fluid and electrolyte levels over the years, periodically requiring close monitoring. Another lab finding showed that his white blood cell count was 9,500/mm^3, falling within a reasonable range, although it did not exclude an underlying infectious or inflammatory process. His GCS score was 12. He also displayed some difficulty maintaining appropriate gas exchange, requiring a moderate level of supplemental oxygen at an FiO2 of 46% to keep his oxygen saturation acceptable. Hematocrit was 30%. Laboratory analysis of arterial blood gases revealed that his partial pressure of oxygen was 74 mm Hg despite this intermediate boost, indicating only partial success in gas transfer. Further details in the same evaluation revealed a distinct acidity in his blood chemistry, with his pH measuring 7.28, well below the midpoint of normal, which could reflect the strain his body was under in trying to eliminate waste products and maintain equilibrium. Although he has a longstanding vulnerability in more than one vital organ system, there is no plan at this moment for an invasive procedure, as current findings do not point to a lesion or process that would benefit from surgical intervention. Clinicians have opted to support him medically, investigate potential triggers for his deteriorating condition, and keep him under intensive observation to ensure that if his blood pressure dips any lower or his breathing worsens, immediate therapeutic actions can be taken. It is of particular concern that his ability to fight off infection or other stressors is reduced, so aggressive monitoring of potential sources of sepsis or other complications is underway. Importantly, no active measures had been initiated before these data were acquired, ensuring that the temperature, cardiovascular parameters, respiratory rates, and laboratory findings accurately reflect his true baseline upon entering the unit. In view of his compromised ability to handle metabolic byproducts, his acid-base disturbance, and his need for moderated oxygen support, his care team is focused on staving off further organ injury and preventing any precipitous drops in alertness while formulating a more definitive diagnosis for his current downturn.

Patient Note:
A 71-year-old female arrived at the intensive care setting late in the evening with a family report of severe weakness, difficulty speaking, and reduced responsiveness that had developed over the preceding day. Prior evaluations in outpatient settings had documented chronic, severe defects in her immune system with markedly reduced lymphocyte counts and recurrent serious infections requiring prolonged antimicrobial therapy, but she had not required recent inpatient services until this rapid deterioration. On admission, she was febrile at 38.9 °C and appeared visibly uncomfortable, exhibiting labored breathing at a rate of 31 breaths per minute. Her blood pressure was checked multiple times, revealing relatively stable systolic readings at 127 mm Hg and diastolic values at 56 mm Hg. Despite these numbers not reflecting dire hemodynamic compromise, her heart rate was notably high at 107 beats per minute, suggesting a correlation with her evident distress. A quick neurological assessment gave her a GCS of 7. The care team, therefore, recognized a major alertness deficit that demanded close observation in the critical care arena. Arterial sampling uncovered a slightly alkalotic status with a pH at 7.47, raising suspicions of a compensatory process or another underlying factor, especially in the setting of increased respiratory effort. Concomitantly, her serum sodium was found to be 132 mEq/L, lying near the lower limit of normal, and her potassium checked in at 3.3 mEq/L, making the clinicians vigilant for possible shifts that could impact cardiac and neuromuscular function. Of particular concern was her elevated indicator of kidney filtration, measured at 2.8 mg/dL, representing new-onset acute renal failure when compared with previously normal renal function in her medical records. Her red blood cell volume percentage was calculated at 47%, indicating that she was not significantly anemic, while her white blood cell concentration of 11,100 per cubic millimeter hinted at a mild inflammatory or infectious trigger without extreme leukocytosis. She was started on a moderate amount of supplemental oxygen, with approximately a little more than a third of the inspired gas containing additional oxygen (FiO₂ at 37%), yet her arterial partial pressure was only 77 mm Hg, prompting concern for impaired gas exchange. Clinicians reviewing her case also took into account that she had recognized vulnerabilities, and plans were made to watch for complications that might arise from infection or organ-specific stress, especially given her susceptible background. Notably, she was not scheduled for any invasive procedure, so the team’s main focus remained on ruling out discreet causes of her confusion, respiratory compromise, and possible systemic insult. Given her significant alteration in consciousness, staff closely monitored her airway and circulation, concerned that this constellation of high temperature, increased pulse, and breathing difficulty could progress unless the root etiology was quickly identified and addressed. Throughout these initial hours, no definitive interventions—beyond ensuring adequate ventilation, obtaining vital laboratory and imaging data, and maintaining hemodynamic stability—had been instituted, as the precise reason for her deteriorating condition was still under investigation. Her low responsiveness continued, with eyes remaining nearly closed and no intelligible verbalizations, underscoring the severity of her situation. The care team prepared for a comprehensive diagnostic effort, including thorough imaging of the lungs and abdomen, cultures of body fluids, and repeated electrolyte checks to see if emerging derangements could be corrected promptly. Continual observation and frequent re-evaluation were prioritized to ensure that, if her hemodynamics shifted or breathing patterns worsened, supportive measures could be escalated without delay. Despite the concerning presentation and the explicit knowledge of her predisposition toward more severe disease courses, there was guarded optimism that aggressively pursuing early investigation and stabilizing measures might avert further decompensation.

Patient Note:
A 61-year-old male arrived at the hospital with increasing shortness of breath, mild confusion, and a two-day history of productive cough. He has a past medical history of controlled hypertension and mild fatty liver disease but denies any recent changes in medication or significant dietary shifts. On initial evaluation, he appeared in moderate respiratory distress, prompting careful assessment of his ability to oxygenate and ventilate. Arterial blood gas sampling revealed his partial pressure of oxygen was 70 mm Hg, concerning given his respiratory complaints. He required advanced airway support on arrival due to insufficient oxygenation on lower levels of supplemental oxygen, and he has been receiving a mixture of inspired gas that surpasses room air concentration to help maintain adequate oxygenation. The inspired oxygen concentration is 40% (FiO₂ 0.40). He remains hemodynamically stable with a systolic pressure of 115 mm Hg and a diastolic pressure of 75 mm Hg, showing no clinical indications of severely decreased perfusion or other concerning circulatory compromise. Physical exam demonstrates that while he opens his eyes spontaneously and can communicate coherently most of the time, he occasionally struggles with more complex tasks, which suggests a mild cognitive deficit rather than a profound alteration in consciousness. On formal assessment, his Glasgow Coma Score was 14. Laboratory data at presentation show a platelet count of 150,000 per microliter, which has decreased slightly compared to his baseline but has not yet fallen into a range typically associated with a high bleeding risk. Additional labs reveal a total bilirubin of 1.8 mg/dL, indicating a mild degree of bilirubin elevation that may be influenced by his known hepatic condition but does not yet show overt signs of severe hepatic dysfunction. Renal function testing indicates a serum creatinine of 1.2 mg/dL, an observation that hovers at the upper boundary of normal for his demographic, and he currently has a daily urine output of about 1500 milliliters, indicating that his kidneys are still generating an adequate volume of urine in the setting of his acute illness. He exhibits no overt signs of severe circulatory compromise, and therefore he has not required continuous intravenous medications specifically aimed at supporting blood pressure or cardiac output. At this time, the clinical team has chosen to monitor his cardiovascular parameters closely, particularly because of his potential for sudden changes in respiratory status and the known risk of progression to sepsis secondary to a suspected pulmonary infection. Despite the mild confusion, he retains an overall favorable prognosis for neurological recovery in the absence of additional insults. Efforts are focused on controlling any underlying infectious etiology through appropriate antimicrobial therapy, maintaining stable ventilatory parameters to improve his oxygen saturation, and providing supportive care to address his mild hepatic and renal concerns. The current plan includes serial reassessment of his neurological status, repeat laboratory tests to track platelet trends and liver enzyme fluctuations, and continued close observation of fluid balance to ensure his urine output remains in a safe range. His clinical status upon arrival underscores the importance of prompt recognition of respiratory compromise in individuals with evidence of possible infection, and his moderate deficits in consciousness along with borderline lab abnormalities warrant vigilant monitoring to prevent further organ dysfunction. Although he remains intubated for now, there is cautious optimism that he will improve with medical management and may be weaned from advanced respiratory support once his oxygenation stabilizes and his condition proves to be on an upward trajectory.

Patient Note:
A 59-year-old male arrived at the hospital complaining of worsening shortness of breath, lightheadedness, and a decline in his usual alertness over the past 48 hours. On formal assessment, his Glasgow Coma Score was 12. He was observed breathing spontaneously upon arrival, with no advanced mechanical assistance in place at that time. Arterial blood gas analysis showed an oxygen partial pressure of 82 mm Hg. He was receiving an inspired oxygen fraction of 32 % (FiO₂ 0.32). His blood pressure readings were notable at 90  mm Hg systolic and 50  mm Hg diastolic, reflecting a pattern of persistently low arterial pressure, accompanied by cool extremities and delayed capillary refill. Clinicians noticed a diminished pulse pressure that further supported the suggestion of compromised cardiovascular perfusion. To maintain adequate circulation, a continuous infusion of norEPINEPHrine was delivered at a rate of 0.05 mcg/kg/min, while other intravenous agents often employed for circulatory support were not administered at this stage. Notably, his heart rate was elevated, consistent with a compensatory tachycardia in response to diminished blood pressure. His respiratory examination revealed symmetric chest movements with moderate accessory muscle use, while auscultation did not uncover significant adventitious sounds such as pronounced rales or wheezing, though mild tachypnea was recognized. The patient did not describe a history of chronic pulmonary conditions, yet he reported recent onset of increasing dyspnea even with minimal exertion at home. In terms of his laboratory results, measured shortly after presentation, his platelet count was 190,000 per microliter, indicating an overall stable hematologic profile without immediate risk of severe thrombocytopenia or significant bleeding. Liver-related parameters showed a total bilirubin of 1.2 mg/dL, lying at the upper boundary of normal for some reference ranges, prompting attention to any possible hepatic impact but not suggesting major hyperbilirubinemia at that point. Renal function testing revealed a serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL, slightly above his reported baseline, suggesting mild to moderate deterioration in kidney performance. This finding was further supported by a record of approximately 800 mL of urine output in the last 24 hours, an amount that was lower than his typical daily volume, raising concern about reduced renal clearance and potential prerenal azotemia or an acute kidney insult. The medical staff noted that he had not undergone any recent procedures requiring nephrotoxic contrast, nor had he taken non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory medications in the previous week. Additional evaluation pointed toward possible triggers for his presentation, including a history of inadequate fluid intake over the previous days and a possible subacute infection, although no explicit source of infection was immediately identified on initial imaging or clinical inspection. Review of systems indicated mild gastrointestinal discomfort, but no sustained episodes of vomiting or diarrhea. His skin examination showed slightly cool extremities and mild mottling on the lower legs, though no overt lesions or rashes were observed. Historically, the patient had experienced borderline blood pressure readings, but he had not been on long‑term antihypertensive therapy. No prior admissions for chronic cardiac or respiratory illnesses were documented. He expressed intermittent chest tightness, though routine cardiac enzymes were within normal limits. Clinical suspicion remained high for a systemic inflammatory etiology, or less likely, an evolving obstructive process that was not clinically overt. Ongoing diagnostic work included a review of abdominal and chest imaging, alongside bacteriologic cultures, to target any hidden source of infection or inflammation. He denied recent travel or exposure to unusual pathogens but reported working in an indoor environment where he had occasional contact with individuals who had flu‑like illnesses. The care team planned to reassess his intravascular volume status carefully and consider fluid resuscitation measures if indicated. Consultation with nephrology was requested given his reduced urine output and slight creatinine elevation, though dialysis was not immediately contemplated in the absence of other indications. Neurologic checks were arranged at regular intervals to track any changes in his level of responsiveness or orientation. The primary concern was to identify and address the root cause of his hypotension, mild respiratory insufficiency, and renal impairment without further compromise to organ systems. As he remained in the evaluation area, the team monitored his oxygen saturation, blood pressure, urine output, and mental status closely, with plans to proceed to a higher‑acuity inpatient setting for vigilant observation and treatment once preliminary investigations were concluded.

Patient Note:
A 66-year-old female presented to the emergency department with increasing fatigue, mild shortness of breath, and discoloration of the sclera. She reported a few days of feeling generally unwell, with no clear precipitating event such as trauma or infection. According to her family, she has been complaining of mild right-sided discomfort beneath her rib cage for the past week and has had decreased appetite. Past medical history includes longstanding hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and mild osteoarthritis. On arrival, she was alert and oriented, speaking coherently without any obvious signs of confusion or disorientation. Her mental status was thoroughly evaluated, and she was observed to be awake, responsive to questions, following commands appropriately, giving a GCS of 15. Further clinical examination revealed that she had stable hemodynamics with a blood pressure reading of 105 mm Hg systolic and 60 mm Hg diastolic, which did not necessitate the use of any intravenous medications to support her circulation. She was afebrile and her heart rate was regular without arrhythmias. Although her breathing was evidently more labored than usual, she was still able to converse without significant difficulty. However, arterial blood gas analysis revealed an initial partial pressure of oxygen at around 95 mm Hg while she was receiving a moderate fraction of inspired oxygen, which stood at 36%, suggesting the need for closer monitoring of her respiratory status. She was placed on continuous positive airwary pressure. Additional blood tests revealed slightly elevated bilirubin at 2.0 mg/dL, potentially indicative of some compromise in hepatic processing, though she did not have a known history of chronic liver disease. Her platelet count was noted to be 210,000 per microliter, which fell within acceptable limits for most standard reference ranges. Although she sustained her blood pressure without chemical support, close monitoring was advised due to her history of hypertension. Her kidney function tests showed a creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL, which raised concerns regarding a possible acute on chronic kidney issue, especially as her daily urine output was measured at around 400 mL, signifying reduced excretory capacity. She reported no recent changes in her medications that could have acutely influenced her kidney function, but further evaluation with renal ultrasound and investigations into her diabetic history were planned to elucidate the etiology of her elevated creatinine and limited urine output. Despite her complaints of fatigue, she denied chest pain or any severe discomfort beyond mild abdominal fullness on the right side beneath the rib cage. Her oxygen saturation was maintained at an acceptable level, and her mental clarity underscored the absence of acute intracranial pathology. She reported no history of major recent illness, no recent hospital admissions, and no known exposure to hepatotoxic substances, although she did admit to occasionally using over-the-counter pain medications for her joint aches. Her appetite had been mildly decreased, and she attributed her general fatigue to a possibly worsening diabetic control, yet her fingerstick glucose readings were reportedly within a reasonable range according to her home log. At the time of presentation, there was no evidence of severe circulatory compromise. She was not receiving intravenous infusions to support blood pressure or augment cardiac output, as her measured parameters remained relatively stable. In summary, her profile featured moderate respiratory concerns warranting supportive therapy with a device that provides a constant flow of pressure to ease her work of breathing, mildly elevated bilirubin, an acceptable platelet count, alert mental status, a stable blood pressure at the time, no requirement for vasoactive medication, elevated creatinine suggestive of renal impairment, and decreased urine output that required further diagnostic workup to determine the chronicity and possible underlying causes. Despite the complexities, she remained hemodynamically stable upon initial evaluation, and her respiratory status, while strained, responded to noninvasive positive airway assistance.

Patient Note:
A 62-year-old female with a history of diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and mild congestive heart failure presented to the emergency department with progressive respiratory distress and confusion over the past several days. She required immediate airway support using mechanical ventillation to deliver oxygen at 45 % concentration. Her PaO2 was 65 mm Hg. She was only intermittently responsive, and on formal neurologic assessment her Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) was 10, based on her ability to open her eyes, offer limited verbal responses, and localize to pain but not consistently follow complex commands. Vital signs included a blood pressure of 70 mm Hg systolic and 50 mm Hg diastolic. Laboratory results at presentation showed a platelet count of 120 000 / µL, indicating a mild reduction, and her total bilirubin level was 2.3 mg/dL, which represented a noticeable elevation when compared to her known baseline. Additional testing revealed that her creatinine was 1.9 mg/dL, supporting the suspicion of impaired kidney function beyond her previously documented mild abnormalities, and her urine output had decreased to 650 mL/day over the past 24 hours, suggesting a decline in renal performance. Given her compromised cardiovascular and respiratory status, she was placed on a moderate infusion of dopamine at  2 mcg/kg/min to support her circulation, and norepinephrine at  0.02 mcg/kg/min to further aid hemodynamic stability. Despite these interventions, she displayed persistent fatigue and disorientation. In the days leading up to her arrival, she had experienced increasing shortness of breath, decreased appetite, and reduced fluid intake, which likely contributed to both her respiratory compromise and evolving metabolic derangements. Physical examination was notable for diminished breath sounds in all lung fields without clear focal consolidation, and she exhibited trace pedal edema. Cardiac auscultation revealed a regular rhythm without significant murmurs, though mild jugular venous distension was observed. The family reported that she had been increasingly somnolent, but they attributed it initially to fatigue and had delayed seeking care. Since her admission, she had undergone preliminary imaging that did not reveal obvious signs of major acute changes, such as large pleural effusions or gross consolidation, though subtle infiltrates could not be ruled out. Her mildly elevated bilirubin was investigated for possible hepatic congestion given her history of heart failure, though other causes such as hemolysis were also considered. Furthermore, her relatively low platelet count raised concern about underlying inflammation or early bone marrow suppression, necessitating closer hematologic monitoring. The administration of intravenous fluids was approached with caution due to her borderline renal status and potential risk for fluid overload. Nonetheless, every effort was made to optimize her ventilation and perfusion, with a focus on stabilizing her oxygen saturation and ensuring adequate organ perfusion. The presence of a creatinine level at 1.9 mg/dL on arrival marked a significant change, prompting inquiries into the timing of her renal deterioration and whether recent infections, medications, or hypoperfusion might have contributed. Of particular importance, her daily urine output of 650 mL suggested ongoing renal compromise that warranted vigilant surveillance. Throughout this evaluation, her sedation requirements were kept minimal so that her mental status could be serially assessed, although her baseline cognition remained at a GCS of 10. Carefully balancing her ventilation parameters, intravenous infusions at the specified rates, and potential need for further diagnostic work formed the backbone of her immediate therapeutic strategy. A detailed plan focusing on stabilizing her respiratory function, guarding against worsening renal impairment, and reevaluating her liver function was set in motion, all while closely observing her neurologic status and platelet trends.
